title: Chapter 12
content:
CHAPTER 12: The Root That Remembers

The vine that had grown from my blood wrapped around my forearm like a living tattoo, pulsating with a warmth that shouldn't have existed in this frozen hell. I'd been standing at the edge of my abyss-touched field for three hours now, watching the corrupted soil reject everything I planted except that single crimson tendril.

"You're staring again," Kael said from behind me. The old hunter had appeared without sound, his boots leaving no prints on the ash-covered ground. "The sun will set soon. The surface-night feeds the abyss."

I didn't turn around. "It moved this morning. Reached toward the east, then stopped."

"Toward the dead city."

"Toward *something*." I pulled up my sleeve, revealing where the vine had embedded itself into my skin. The connection was deeper than physical—I could feel the abyss creatures moving in the layers beneath us, could taste their hunger like copper on my tongue. "The book said blood-vines only grow where the abyss has been wounded."

Kael stepped beside me, his weathered face unreadable. "And what wounded it here, delivery boy?"

That was the question that had been eating at me since I woke up in this nightmare. My great-grandfather's journal spoke of a time when humans and abyss creatures existed in balance. Then something changed. The surface became a wasteland. The abyss began consuming everything above.

"The book mentions a 'First Cut,'" I said. "Something that happened five hundred years ago. But the pages after that are torn out."

"Convenient."

"Or someone wanted to make sure I couldn't fix this without knowing the whole story."

The vine pulsed again, stronger this time. I felt it—a pull from the east, like a thread drawing me toward something ancient and waiting. My hand moved before I could stop it, fingers tracing the path the vine wanted to take.

"There's a ruin in the dead city," Kael said slowly. "The old ones called it the Root Temple. They said it was built before the First Cut, back when the abyss was just another layer of the world, not a wound that wouldn't heal."

"Why haven't you gone there?"

"Because nothing that enters the dead city returns unchanged. The abyss doesn't just kill—it *remembers*. And what it remembers of humans isn't kind."

I looked down at my hands. The vine's crimson glow had spread into my veins, creating a web of light beneath my skin. I wasn't just carrying abyss-touched blood anymore. I was becoming part of its network, a node in something vast and hungry.

"Then I'll go alone."

"The creatures will sense you the moment you cross the city's boundary."

"Let them." I grabbed my pack, checking the supplies I'd scavenged over the past week. Salt. Iron nails. Dried meat. A single page from the journal that had survived the tearing—a diagram of a root system that spread across the entire continent, connecting surface to abyss in ways that shouldn't have been possible. "I'm not going to fight them."

Kael's voice hardened. "Then what are you going to do?"

I held up the vine-wrapped arm. "I'm going to show them something they've never seen before. A human who can speak their language."

The walk to the dead city took four hours through terrain that shifted every time I blinked. One moment I was crossing dried riverbeds filled with bones that looked human but weren't. The next I was wading through fields of fungus that released spores carrying whispers of forgotten languages.

The vine guided me. It knew the path even when my eyes couldn't see it, pulling me left when the ground looked solid but would have dropped me into a nest of shadow-worms, urging me forward when every instinct screamed to turn back.

By the time I reached the city's edge, the sun had become a bruised purple bruise on the horizon. The buildings before me weren't ruins in the traditional sense—they were *grown*, not built. Walls of calcified root and bone rose in spiraling patterns that hurt to look at directly. Windows pulsed with internal light, like eyes watching from the dark.

"The temple is at the center," I muttered, repeating what the vine had been telling me for hours. "Beneath the spire that doesn't cast a shadow."

I stepped through the city's threshold.

The air changed immediately. It became thick, heavy with something that pressed against my skin like a physical weight. The whispers I'd heard in the fungus fields became voices—distinct, angry, *hungry*.

*Human flesh. Human blood. Human lies.*

The words crawled through my skull. I gripped the vine tighter, and it flared bright enough to cast shadows.

"I'm not here to fight," I said aloud, my voice echoing through empty streets. "I'm here to understand."

The city answered with silence. Then the ground beneath my feet began to move.

Roots burst from the earth, not attacking but *examining*. They wrapped around my ankles, my wrists, my throat. The vine on my arm pulsed in response, and I felt the moment of recognition pass between them—the realization that I carried abyss within me, that I was something new, something that shouldn't exist.

*The wound-walker returns.*

The voice came from everywhere and nowhere. It resonated in my bones, in the marrow the vine had claimed as its own.

"I'm not returning," I said. "I'm arriving for the first time."

*Lies. We remember your blood. We remember your great-grandfather's hands in our soil, planting the seeds that would become our prison.*

The roots tightened. I couldn't breathe.

"Then tell me what he did. Tell me why the pages were torn."

*Because the truth would kill you. Because knowing what he planted would force you to choose—and humans have never chosen well.*

The vine on my arm burned. Not with pain, but with something like recognition. Like it had been waiting for this conversation.

"You're wrong," I gasped. "I'm already choosing. I'm choosing to know."

The roots held me suspended for what felt like hours. Then they released, dropping me to the ground in a heap. The voice returned, softer now, almost curious.

*Then follow the blood-vine. It will show you what your great-grandfather buried. But know this, wound-walker: some roots should never be unearthed. Some truths are abyss for a reason.*

I stood, brushing ash from my clothes. The street ahead of me had changed—where there had been walls of root and bone, now there was a path leading down, spiraling into darkness that swallowed light itself.

The vine pulled. I followed.

The descent took me beneath the city, into chambers that predated human memory. Walls covered in carvings that showed the abyss not as a wound but as a garden—tended by figures that were neither human nor creature, but something in between. They planted seeds in the darkness, harvested fruits of pure shadow, maintained a balance that kept both worlds alive.

Then the carvings changed. Human figures appeared, armed with tools that looked like mine. They dug into the abyss's soil, tore out roots that connected the layers, stole the fruits of shadow for their own purposes.

And at the center of this destruction stood a figure I recognized from my great-grandfather's journal—a young man with my face, my build, my hands stained with abyss-blood as he harvested something that glowed with stolen light.

"That's me," I whispered. "But that's impossible."

*Nothing is impossible in the garden of the dead.*

The voice came from behind me. I turned to face a creature that defied description—a mass of roots and eyes and mouths that spoke in harmony, each word a different tone, a different age, a different truth.

"I didn't do this. I haven't even been here for a month."

*Time in the abyss is not time on the surface. Your great-grandfather's hands, your hands, the hands of every human who ever wounded the balance—they are all the same. You are not the first wound-walker. You are the latest iteration of an old curse.*

The vine burned hot enough to scar. I felt it—the memories flooding through the connection. Not my memories. *Its* memories. The abyss's memory of every human who had ever entered it, every betrayal, every broken promise.

But beneath the anger, I felt something else. A thread of hope, buried so deep it had almost been forgotten.

"There's a way to fix this," I said. "That's why the book found me. That's why the vine chose me."

The creature of roots and eyes tilted what passed for its head. *There is a way. But it requires what your kind has never been willing to give.*

"What?"

*The first harvest. The one your great-grandfather stole. It must be returned to the abyss's heart.*

I looked at the carvings again. The figure with my face, holding the glowing fruit. The first harvest. The seed that had started everything.

"And if I do that?"

*Then the wound begins to heal. The surface regenerates. The abyss retreats to its proper depths.*

*And you become part of it.*

The vine pulsed one final time, and I understood. Returning the first harvest wasn't just about giving back what had been stolen. It was about replacing it with something of equal value.

The price was me.

I stood in the darkness of the Root Temple, surrounded by five hundred years of history written in blood and root and bone, and made my choice.

"When do we start?"

The creature's many mouths smiled.

title: Chapter 13
content:
CHAPTER 13: The Vine That Remembers

The dawn came gray and heavy over Green Fortune, the clouds pressing down like a lid on a pot. I stood at the edge of the terraced fields, watching the abyssal wheat sway in a breeze that smelled of wet stone and something else—something metallic, like blood left too long in the sun.

The growth had been explosive. Three days since I'd planted the seeds we harvested from the first batch, and already the stalks stood waist-high. The soil around them had turned the color of old bruises, veins of dark purple spreading through the earth like a disease. Or a blessing. I still couldn't tell which.

"You're up early."

I didn't turn around. Lin Mei's footsteps were soft, but I'd learned to recognize the rhythm of her walk—the slight drag of her left foot where an old injury had never quite healed.

"Couldn't sleep," I said. "The wheat's growing faster than I calculated."

She came to stand beside me, her qipao a splash of deep blue against the gray morning. The jade pendant at her throat caught the weak light, throwing a green flash across her face.

"How much faster?"

"Yesterday it was knee-high. Today it's waist-high. At this rate, we'll have a full harvest in another three days."

"That's good, isn't it?"

I shook my head. "Nothing in the abyss grows this fast without a price. Look at the soil."

She crouched down, her fingers brushing the dark earth. When she stood, there was a faint purple residue on her fingertips. She rubbed them together, frowning.

"It's spreading."

"Yeah. And I don't know where it's drawing from."

The book had been silent since we returned from the hollow grove. Not literally—the pages still showed me what I needed to see—but the voice, that ancient whisper at the edge of my thoughts, had gone quiet. Like it was waiting for something.

Or someone.

"The traders are getting nervous," Lin Mei said. "They can see the changes from the village. Some of them are talking about leaving."

"Let them go. We don't need loose tongues spreading rumors about what we're doing here."

"Zhang Wei won't leave. He's been asking to see the fields."

I turned to face her fully. "What did you tell him?"

"That it wasn't my decision to make."

The sun had begun to break through the clouds, shafts of golden light cutting through the mist. It caught the wheat in a way that made it look almost beautiful—the deep purple of the stalks, the black grain heavy on the stems, the way the plants seemed to lean toward me as I walked past.

That was the part that bothered me most. The leaning.

"Bring him at noon," I said. "I'll have the new batch of fertilizer ready. If he's serious about helping, he can haul dirt like everyone else."

Lin Mei smiled, but it didn't reach her eyes. "And if he's not?"

"Then he's not, and we've lost nothing but a morning."

She left to prepare breakfast, and I turned back to the fields. The vines from the hollow grove had grown as well, threading through the wheat stalks like green veins through flesh. They pulsed with a faint light, a rhythm that almost matched my heartbeat.

I'd brought them here because they seemed to thrive in the abyssal soil, because their fruit had sustained us during the worst days. But now I wondered if I'd brought something else with them. Something that was waking up.

---

Noon came with the heat. The mist had burned off, leaving the air thick and humid. Zhang Wei arrived with two pack mules and a cart full of supplies—rice, dried meat, salt, and a crate of something he wouldn't let anyone open until I'd seen it.

He was a big man, broad-shouldered and weathered, with the kind of face that had seen a lot of hard years and didn't expect any easy ones. When he shook my hand, his grip was firm but not challenging.

"Thank you for seeing me, Mr. Chen."

"Just Chen," I said. "Or Zhao. Take your pick."

"Chen, then." He looked past me at the fields, and I watched his expression shift from curiosity to unease. "You've done... something here."

"I've farmed. That's all."

"That's not all, and we both know it." He let out a long breath. "I've been trading at the border for fifteen years. I've seen what comes out of the abyss. I've seen what happens to people who go in and don't come back. But I've never seen anything like this."

"What exactly are you asking, Zhang Wei?"

He turned to face me, and there was something in his eyes that I hadn't expected—fear, yes, but also hope. A desperate, fragile hope that he was trying to keep contained.

"I'm asking if you've found a way to make the abyss farmable. A way to grow food in the poisoned soil. A way to feed the villages that are starving because the surface lands are dying."

The words hit me harder than I expected. I'd been so focused on survival, on figuring out the mechanics of abyssal agriculture, that I'd forgotten what it could mean for others.

"It's not that simple," I said. "The plants we're growing here, they need something from the abyss. Something that's not on the surface."

"What?"

I held up the book. "I'm still learning."

Zhang Wei's eyes widened when he saw the cover—the strange symbols that writhed like living things, the way the leather seemed to shift in the light. "That book. Where did you get it?"

"Great-grandfather."

"Your great-grandfather was Chen Feng?"

The name hit me like a punch to the chest. "You knew him?"

"I knew of him. Everyone at the border knew of him. He was the first person to go into the abyss and come back with something other than death. He brought back seeds. Plants that could grow in the poisoned soil. But he never told anyone where he found them, and when he died, the knowledge died with him."

I opened the book to a random page, and for the first time in days, the words shifted. They formed new sentences, new diagrams, new instructions that made my blood run cold.

*The seeds remember. The soil remembers. The abyss remembers everything that has been planted in it, and everything that has been taken from it. To grow the harvest is to awaken the past.*

"What does it say?" Zhang Wei asked.

I closed the book. "It says we need to be careful."

---

That night, I couldn't sleep. The words from the book kept running through my mind, and the vines had begun to twitch in ways that made my skin crawl. I went out to the fields with a lantern, walking between the rows of wheat that now reached my chest.

The purple soil had spread beyond the terraces, creeping into the surrounding forest. The trees nearest to the fields had begun to change—their bark taking on the same dark hue, their leaves turning black and glossy like the abyssal grain.

I stopped when I reached the center of the field, where the vines from the hollow grove had formed a kind of nest. A structure of green and purple that pulsed with that same faint light, that same rhythm that matched my heartbeat.

And in the center of the nest, something was growing.

It looked like a fruit, at first—a bulbous shape the size of my head, covered in the same black sheen as the grain. But as I got closer, I saw that it wasn't a fruit at all. It was a pod. A cocoon. Something wrapped in layers of abyssal matter, waiting to be born.

The book fell open in my hands, and the words burned across the page:

*The first harvest yields the first awakening. What sleeps beneath will rise to claim what was planted.*

I reached out to touch the pod, and the vines wrapped around my wrist. Not painfully, but firmly. Holding me in place.

A crack appeared on the surface of the pod. Then another. Then the whole thing split open, and I saw what was inside.

A hand. Human-shaped, but wrong—the fingers too long, the skin too pale, the nails black and sharp. It twitched, grasping at the air, and then another hand burst through, and I was scrambling backward, pulling against the vines that held me, my heart hammering against my ribs.

The pod tore open completely, and a figure emerged.

It looked like a man. Naked, hairless, with eyes that were solid black from lid to lid. It stood on unsteady legs, its head swiveling as it took in its surroundings. When its gaze landed on me, it stopped.

And then it spoke.

"You are the planter."

The voice was wrong—too deep, too resonant, like it came from a throat that wasn't meant for human speech.

"I'm... I'm Zhao Chen."

"We know who you are." It took a step toward me, and the vines released my wrist. "We have been waiting for you."

"Waiting? Who's 'we'?"

The creature—the abyssal-born, the thing that had grown from my seeds and my soil and my blood—tilted its head, and something like a smile spread across its too-wide mouth.

"The ones who were planted before you. The ones who will be planted after. The abyss is not empty, planter. It is a garden. And you have only just begun to tend it."

I backed away, my hand going to the knife at my belt. "What do you want?"

"To help you. To guide you. The book cannot teach you everything. There are secrets that only we remember, secrets that were buried when your great-grandfather took the seeds and fled."

"You knew Chen Feng?"

"We were his harvest. His first harvest. He planted us in the deep soil, and we grew, and we watched him leave." The creature's voice hardened. "And we have been waiting ever since for someone to return."

I looked at the fields around me—the waving wheat, the pulsing vines, the dark soil that had spread beyond my control. And I realized that I hadn't been building a farm.

I'd been opening a door.

"How many of you are there?" I asked.

The creature's smile widened. "All of them. Every seed you plant, every vine you grow, every harvest you reap—they are all us. We are the abyss, planter. And you have invited us into your world."

I turned to run, but the vines had grown while I wasn't looking. They wrapped around my ankles, my knees, my waist. They pulled me down into the soil, and I felt the purple earth closing over my feet, my legs, my chest.

The last thing I saw before the darkness took me was the creature's black eyes, watching me with something that might have been hunger.

And then I was under.

title: Chapter 14
content:
CHAPTER 14: The Price of Growth

The first sign of trouble came at dawn.

Chen Wei woke to find his vine trembling. Not the usual morning stretch toward the abyss light, but a violent shudder that sent ripples through the soil. The pearly fruits along its length had turned gray overnight, their surfaces cracked like dried mud.

"What the hell?"

He scrambled up, nearly tripping over Xiao Bai. The goat stood rigid, ears flat, staring at the vine with eyes that reflected the gray sky above.

The second sign came from the abyss itself. A sound like grinding bones, rising from below. Not the usual growls and hisses, but something rhythmic. Intentional.

Lin Mei appeared at his side, spear already drawn. "It's been doing that since midnight. I couldn't wake you."

"You should have—"

"You needed sleep." She cut him off. "You've been pushing too hard."

Chen Wei wanted to argue, but the vine shuddered again, and a fruit the size of his fist dropped to the ground. It hit the soil with a wet thud and cracked open, revealing nothing inside but gray dust.

"Three more gone in the last hour," Lin Mei said quietly. "The others are turning."

Chen Wei touched the nearest fruit. Its surface crumbled under his fingers, releasing a fine powder that smelled of copper and rot. The vine's blood-red leaves had begun to curl at the edges, their veins turning black.

He pulled out the notebook, flipping through pages he'd memorized weeks ago. Great-grandfather's handwriting covered everything from soil composition to harvest timing, but nowhere did it mention this. Nowhere did it explain why a healthy abyss vine would start dying from the inside out.

Unless.

He looked at the abyss edge. The sound had grown louder, closer. Like something crawling up the walls.

"Stay here," he said.

"Like hell—"

"Lin Mei." He grabbed her shoulders. "If something comes out of that abyss, you need to be ready to run. Not fight. Run."

Her jaw tightened, but she nodded.

Chen Wei approached the edge alone. The abyss light pulsed below, casting long shadows across the chasm walls. He'd come to recognize its rhythm—a heartbeat that matched the vine's growth cycles. But now that heartbeat was wrong. Irregular. Stuttering like a dying engine.

He looked down.

The walls were moving.

Not rocks. Not shadows. Something else. Something that had been there all along, hidden in the darkness between the light pulses. And now it was climbing.

Chen Wei's blood ran cold as he recognized the shapes. Vines. Thousands of them, covering every inch of the abyss walls below. They moved like snakes, sliding upward inch by inch, their surfaces covered in the same pearly fruits that hung from his own vine.

But these fruits weren't gray. They were red. Deep, pulsing red that glowed with their own sickly light.

"What are you?" he whispered.

The vines answered.

They stopped moving. Every single one, frozen in place. And then they turned toward him.

Not toward his voice. Toward him. As if they'd heard him, understood him, and were now paying attention.

Chen Wei stumbled back from the edge.

The vines surged upward.

He ran.

"Get back!" he shouted. "Get everyone—"

The ground shook. A crack split the earth behind him, racing from the abyss edge toward his farm. His vine screamed—an actual scream, high and piercing, that drilled into his skull.

Xiao Bai bolted. Lin Mei grabbed his arm, dragging him toward the hut.

"What's happening?" she yelled over the noise.

"I don't know! It's like they're—"

The vine tore itself from the ground.

Not ripped out. Not broken. It pulled its roots from the soil like a man pulling his feet from mud. The earth churned, and the vine rose on a tangle of blood-red roots that had grown deeper than Chen Wei had ever checked.

They'd been wrong. All of them. The vine wasn't just growing. It was rooted. Tied to something below. And now it was coming up.

"Chen Wei!" Lin Mei's voice cut through his shock.

He turned to see the vine's main trunk twisting, splitting open along its length. From the gap spilled not sap, but water. Dark water that steamed when it hit the ground.

Blood.

The vine was bleeding.

And from the wound, something emerged.

At first Chen Wei thought it was a hand. Pale, slender fingers reaching out of the gash. Then the arm followed, then the shoulder, then the head.

A woman.

She stepped out of the vine as if stepping through a door, completely naked, her skin the color of moonlight on water. Her hair was black as the abyss, falling past her waist in strands that moved on their own, reaching toward the ground like hungry roots.

Her eyes were white. No pupils, no irises. Just white.

She looked at Chen Wei, and he felt something cold run down his spine.

"You," she said. Her voice was wrong. It came from inside his head, not from her lips. "You woke me."

"Woke—who are you?"

"I am the root." She raised her hand, and the vine behind her twisted, growing, reshaping into a throne of thorns and leaves. "I am the first growth. The mother of your little garden."

Lin Mei had her spear up, but her hands were shaking. "Stay back."

The woman ignored her. Her empty eyes stayed fixed on Chen Wei. "You planted my seed. You fed it blood. You gave it purpose." She smiled, and her lips didn't quite match the expression. "You have no idea what you've done."

"Then tell me."

"You've grown a bridge." She gestured at the vine, now towering above them, its fruits blackening and falling like hail. "A connection between two worlds. The surface and the abyss. Light and dark."

"The book said the vine could purify the abyss—"

"The book lies." Her smile widened. "Or rather, it told half the truth. The vine does purify. It draws corruption from the abyss and transforms it into growth. But growth requires fuel. And the abyss has been growing for a very, very long time."

Chen Wei's mind raced. "The fruits. They're not just food."

"No." She stepped closer, and the ground where she walked turned gray, then black. "They're reservoirs. Each one holds a piece of the abyss's hunger. And when they ripen, when they're eaten..." She tilted her head. "They don't just feed the body. They feed the dark."

"I've been eating them. Lin Mei's been eating them."

"Yes." Her voice was almost gentle now. "And now you carry the abyss inside you. Both of you. Every bite, every harvest, every meal—you've been planting seeds of darkness in your own blood."

Lin Mei grabbed his arm. "She's lying. She's trying to scare us."

"She's not lying." Chen Wei's voice came out hollow. "That's why I've been sleeping better. Why my dreams are fading. It's not peace. It's corruption. The abyss is filling the empty spaces."

The woman laughed, and the sound cracked the ground. "Smart. But you're still thinking too small. You think this is about you? About two humans carrying a little darkness?" She spread her arms, and the vine behind her exploded into motion, branches whipping out in every direction. "This is about the abyss learning to grow. Learning to reach. Learning to climb."

She pointed at the vine, now a writhing tower of thorns and blood. "You've given it a ladder. A path from the depths to the surface. And now it's coming."

The ground shook harder. From the abyss below, a roar rose that dwarfed everything Chen Wei had heard before. It wasn't a beast. It wasn't a storm. It was the abyss itself, waking up.

"The corruption you've been fighting," the woman said, her white eyes gleaming, "the monsters, the darkness, the poison in the soil—that was just the abyss dreaming. You've made it conscious. You've given it a name. You've given it a door."

Chen Wei's hands clenched into fists. "Then I'll burn it. The vine, the farm, everything. I'll start over."

"No." The woman's voice hardened. "You can't. Because you don't understand yet." She stepped forward, and suddenly she was right in front of him, close enough to touch. Her hand rose, and her fingers brushed his cheek.

He felt it. The cold. The hunger. The endless, patient darkness that waited beneath his skin.

"The seed isn't in the vine anymore," she whispered. "It's in you."

She drew back her hand, and where she'd touched him, a black mark remained. A spiral, like a tiny abyss, spinning on his cheek.

Lin Mei lunged, spear aimed at the woman's heart.

The woman caught it with two fingers. She smiled, and the spear shattered into splinters.

"Don't worry," she said, turning away. "You have time. A few days, maybe. The corruption hasn't taken hold yet. But it will."

She walked toward the vine, and the thorns parted to receive her. "You wanted to save the world, farmer. You wanted to bridge the abyss and the surface." She looked back over her shoulder, and her white eyes held a terrible amusement. "Congratulations. You succeeded."

She dissolved into the vine, and the wound sealed behind her.

The vine fell still.

The roaring from the abyss stopped.

And Chen Wei stood in the middle of his ruined farm, a black spiral burning on his face, carrying an infection he couldn't cut out.

Lin Mei grabbed his shoulders. "We'll find a way. We'll—"

"Look at the vine."

She turned.

The vine was changing. The gray fruits had all fallen, but new ones were growing. Red ones. Pulsing with the same sickly light as the vines in the abyss.

And at the top, where the woman had entered, a bud was forming. Not a leaf. Not a fruit. A flower.

A flower that opened to reveal an eye.

It looked down at them. Blinked once. And smiled.

Chen Wei's hand went to his cheek, where the spiral burned like a brand.

"Lin Mei," he said quietly. "How long have I been eating those fruits?"

She hesitated. "Two weeks. Since the first harvest."

"Both of us?"

"Yes."

He looked at her, really looked. And for the first time, he noticed the faint gray at the edges of her eyes. The same gray he'd been ignoring in his own reflection.

"How long," he said, "before we turn into her?"

Lin Mei didn't answer.

But the eye on the vine did.

"Soon," it said, in the woman's voice. "Very soon."

And below, from the abyss, new sounds began to rise.

Not roars.

Footsteps. Thousands of footsteps, climbing the ladder he'd built.

Chen Wei opened his notebook, hands shaking. He'd find an answer. He had to.

But the final pages were blank.

And his great-grandfather's last entry, the one he'd always dismissed as madness, stared up at him in trembling handwriting:

*"If you read this, I failed. The vine grows. The abyss climbs. And you carry the seed of both worlds.*

*There is only one cure.*

*The root must die.*

*Even if you die with it."*

title: Chapter 15
content:
CHAPTER 15: The Seed That Remembers

The thing about farming in the abyss—you never really got used to the smell. That rotten-egg sulfur mixed with wet copper, the way it clung to your clothes and hair no matter how many times you washed. Three weeks since I'd first fallen through that crack in reality, and I still couldn't shake it.

But I was getting used to other things.

Like the way my bloodvine pulsed under my palm as I walked the perimeter of the garden. Like the soft hum that vibrated through the soil when the mooncap mushrooms were ready to harvest. Like the distant rumble that meant something big was moving in the deep tunnels below.

"What do you mean, 'something big'?" I asked, stopping mid-stride.

The vine wrapped around my wrist, tugging gently toward the eastern wall. Since it had grown from my own blood, we'd developed... well, I wouldn't call it conversation. More like a shared nervous system. It didn't think in words, but it had opinions. Strong ones.

"Bigger than the crawlers?"

A sharp pull. Yes.

"Bigger than the tunnel worm?"

Another pull, this one almost impatient.

I swallowed. "Bigger than the thing that made that ten-foot crater?"

The vine went still, then slowly unwound from my wrist. It slithered across the ground, weaving through my carefully planted rows of glowmoss and crystalshrooms, until it reached the eastern wall. There it stopped, pressing its tip against the stone like a finger to lips.

*Listen.*

I pressed my ear to the cool rock. At first, nothing—just the constant drip of moisture somewhere deeper in the cave system. But then, beneath it, a rhythm. Not footsteps. Something slower. More deliberate.

*Thump. Thump. Thump.*

Like a heartbeat. But wrong. Off-beat, like it had forgotten its own rhythm.

"How far down?"

The vine traced a circle on the stone, then drew a line through it. *Close.*

"Great. Just great." I ran my hand through my hair, feeling the grime that had become permanent. "Okay. Priorities. The mooncaps are ready. The bloodvine's producing like crazy. I've got enough essence to maybe—*maybe*—make another purchase from whatever weird system runs this place."

I pulled out my great-grandfather's journal, the leather cover cracked and warm. The pages had started doing something strange lately—words appeared and disappeared depending on where I was standing. When I'd first fallen, it had shown me how to plant the seed. Now, the ink shifted like living shadows.

*The abyss remembers,* the latest entry read. *And what remembers can be harvested.*

"Thanks, Great-Grandpa. Super helpful."

I'd been farming in this hole for three weeks. In that time, I'd learned:

1. Mooncap mushrooms glow brighter the more fear they absorb. Useful for light. Terrifying implication.
2. Bloodvine grows fastest when fed essence from killed abyss creatures. It also gets... aggressive.
3. The deeper you go, the less the rules of the surface apply.

I'd been sticking to the upper levels, maybe fifty feet below the surface crack. Close enough to see pale daylight filtering through the crevice. Far enough that the abyss creatures couldn't reach—or so I'd thought.

The thumping stopped.

I waited, breath held, counting seconds. Ten. Twenty. Nothing.

"False alarm," I whispered.

That's when the wall cracked.

Not a big crack. Hairline, barely visible in the glowmoss light. But it started at the floor and climbed upward, slow and deliberate, like a wound opening.

The bloodvine coiled around my ankle, pulling me back.

"Okay, okay, I'm moving."

I grabbed my harvesting basket—the one I'd woven from vine fibers—and started packing mooncaps as fast as I could. They were my main source of light essence, and light was the only thing that kept the deeper abyss creatures at bay. Without them, I'd be blind. And blind in the abyss meant dead.

The crack widened. A sliver of darkness seeped through, blacker than the cave around me. It didn't reflect light. It *ate* it.

"That's new."

The vine tugged harder.

"Yeah, yeah, I'm going."

But I couldn't help staring. The darkness in the crack wasn't empty. Something moved within it. Something that watched.

I'd seen a lot of things in three weeks. Crawlers with too many legs. Worms that dissolved rock. Floating jellyfish-things that sang songs that made your eyes bleed. But this was different.

This was *patient*.

I grabbed the last of the mooncaps and ran.

---

The garden wasn't much—maybe twenty feet across, carved out of the cave floor with nothing but a stolen pickaxe and desperation. But it was mine. I'd built it from nothing. Planted the first seeds with shaking hands, watered them with my own sweat, and watched them grow in soil that should have been toxic.

And now, standing at the center of it, I could feel the progress.

The bloodvine had spread along the walls, forming a living net that pulsed with essence. The mooncaps had multiplied, their soft blue glow pushing back the shadows. The crystalshrooms had started forming patterns in the dirt, geometric shapes that I didn't understand but that felt important.

But the system—whatever it was that let me trade essence for new seeds—hadn't given me anything new in days.

*Current essence: 847 units*
*Available purchases:*
*- Abyssal Radish seeds (300 essence)*
*- Void Wheat grain (500 essence)*
*- Crimson Lotus root (1000 essence)*
*- ??? (??? essence)*

The question marks had been there since I'd first seen the list. Three days ago, I'd had exactly 1000 essence. Now I had 847. Something was draining it while I slept.

I turned to the bloodvine. "You know anything about this?"

It rippled, which I'd learned meant *maybe*.

"Great. My own plants are keeping secrets."

I knelt beside the nearest mooncap cluster, pressing my fingers into the soil. Warm. Alive. The essence flowed through it like a second bloodstream, connecting everything in the garden. I closed my eyes and *listened*.

The hum was there, same as always. But beneath it, a whisper. Faint. Like someone talking through a wall.

*...can't hold much longer...*

*...he doesn't know what he planted...*

*...the seed remembers...*

My eyes snapped open. "Who's there?"

Silence. The bloodvine had gone still, its tendrils frozen mid-sway. The mooncaps dimmed. Even the distant drip of water had stopped.

"Okay. That's not freaky at all."

I stood, brushing dirt off my knees. The crack in the eastern wall was still there, but the darkness had receded. For now.

I had a decision to make.

I could save up for the Crimson Lotus root—whatever that was—or I could take the safe bet and buy more of what I knew worked. The radish seeds would grow fast, provide food and essence. The wheat would take longer but yield more.

But the question marks... I couldn't stop thinking about them.

"What do you think?" I asked the vine.

It slithered up my arm, wrapping around my shoulder, and pointed a tendril toward the crack in the wall.

*That way.*

"Into the darkness?"

A firm tug.

"You want me to go deeper."

Another tug.

"I don't have a weapon. I don't have armor. I don't have—"

The vine tightened, almost painfully, and I felt something press into my palm. A seed. Not one I'd planted. Dark purple, almost black, with veins of silver running through it like lightning trapped in glass.

"Where did you get this?"

The vine didn't answer. It just pulsed, warm and urgent, and I understood.

*Plant it. Now.*

I looked at the seed in my hand. Looked at the crack in the wall, where the darkness waited. Looked at my garden, my fragile little patch of life in a dead world.

"What the hell," I muttered. "It's not like I have a better plan."

I dug a hole with my bare hands, dropped the seed in, and covered it. Then I poured what little water I had left over the soil.

Nothing happened.

Of course nothing happened. Seeds don't just— 

The ground shook.

Not like the thumping from before. This was deeper. More fundamental. Like the abyss itself was waking up.

The soil cracked open.

And something *grew*.

Not slowly, like normal plants. This thing *erupted*, tearing through the dirt with a hunger that made my blood run cold. Stems thick as my arm, leaves black as void, and in the center—

A flower.

But wrong. So wrong. It had too many petals, arranged in patterns that hurt to look at. They opened and closed, opened and closed, like a mouth tasting the air.

And when it turned toward me, I felt it *see* me.

"Okay. That's definitely new."

The bloodvine tightened around my arm, and I felt its fear. Its *jealousy*.

"You knew this would happen, didn't you?"

The vine rippled. *Yes.*

"Why didn't you tell me?"

Silence.

The flower opened wider, and a voice emerged. Not from the air, but from inside my skull. Soft. Old. Hungry.

*Finally.*

I stumbled backward, but the plant's roots had already spread, wrapping around my ankles. Not painfully. Gently. Like it was holding me in place.

*You have been cultivating in the dark, little rancher. Planting seeds you do not understand. Growing life in dead soil.*

"Who are you?"

*I am what you planted. I am what has always been planted. I am the first seed, the last harvest, the memory of the abyss made flesh.*

"That's... not reassuring."

The flower laughed, a sound like breaking glass. *You should be afraid. But you should also be grateful.*

"Why's that?"

*Because I am the only thing in this place that can teach you what you truly are.*

The roots tightened, and the flower leaned closer, its petals brushing against my face. Where they touched, my skin tingled. Burned. Transformed.

*You are not a farmer, little rancher.*

"Then what am I?"

The flower opened wider, revealing something inside. A seed. No bigger than my thumb. But pulsing with a light that made the mooncaps look like dying embers.

*You are the harvest.*

The ground split open beneath me, and I fell.

Down into darkness.

Down into the heart of the abyss.

And the last thing I saw, before the light vanished completely, was my garden—my beautiful, impossible garden—being swallowed by roots.

title: Chapter 16
content:
CHAPTER 16: The Price of Growth

The vine wrapped around my forearm pulsed with heat, each throb sending waves of pain up to my shoulder. I gritted my teeth, watching as the crimson strands burrowed deeper into my skin, searching for something I couldn't name.

"Stop struggling," Kael said, his voice flat. "You'll make it worse."

"Easy for you to say." I gasped as another wave hit. "This thing's trying to eat me alive."

We were in the medical bay of the surface garrison—a stone room lit by phosphorescent crystals that cast everything in sickly green light. The abyss herbs I'd harvested over the past week lined the walls, their bioluminescent glow mixing with the crystals to create a pattern that made my eyes water.

Lysander stood by the door, his hand resting on his sword hilt. "The binding ritual should have worked. The vine recognized you as its master."

"Then why does it feel like it's trying to replace my blood with fire?" I tried to pull away, but Kael's grip on my wrist was like iron.

"Because you've been feeding it wrong." The herbalist's weathered face twisted into something like concern. "You've been giving it water. Sunlight. Surface nutrients."

"Isn't that what plants need?"

"Not abyss plants." He released my arm and walked to a shelf lined with clay jars. "They require a different kind of sustenance. Something the abyss provides in abundance, but the surface lacks entirely."

Lysander stepped forward. "What are you saying?"

Kael stopped, his back to us. "I'm saying your rancher here has been starving his vine. And now it's trying to take what it needs directly from his life force."

The room went cold. I looked down at my arm, at the veins of crimson spreading from the vine's attachment point. They'd reached my elbow now, branching like a tree of blood beneath my skin.

"How long do I have?" My voice came out steadier than I felt.

"Three days. Maybe four if you stop using it entirely." Kael turned, holding a small ceramic pot. "Or we can fix this. But the solution won't be pleasant."

I laughed, the sound hollow in the stone room. "Nothing about this world has been pleasant. Give me the bad news."

"The vine needs to feed on abyss essence. Pure, concentrated abyss essence." He set the pot on the table between us. "The only source strong enough to sustain it is the corruption itself."

"You want me to infect my own farm."

"I want you to survive."

Lysander moved between us. "That's madness. The corruption spreads. It consumes. If he introduces it to his land—"

"He's already introduced it." Kael's eyes locked onto mine. "That vine came from the abyss. Every time it grows, every time it bears fruit, it's pulling energy from somewhere. Right now, it's pulling from you. But if you give it a proper source..."

"You're talking about a permanent connection. One that would make me a conduit between surface and abyss."

"I'm talking about making you the first person to truly bridge both worlds."

Silence stretched between us. I could feel the vine pulsing, hungry, demanding. Outside, I could hear the screams of the garrison drills, soldiers preparing for an invasion that everyone knew was coming.

"What about the other plants?" I asked. "The herbs. The blazethorn. Will they need the same treatment?"

Kael's expression didn't change. "Some will. Some are already adapted to surface conditions. But anything you want to be truly powerful, anything that could make a real difference in this war... it will need the abyss."

I thought about my farm. The careful rows of herbs I'd planted. The wall I'd reinforced with abyss-hardened wood. The fields I'd imagined feeding an army.

I thought about the refugees in the lower district. About the children who'd never seen sunlight. About the surface nobles who'd rather burn the entire city than admit their savior came from below.

"What would it cost?" I asked. "To make the connection."

Kael's hand hovered over the pot. "You'd need to plant a seed of corruption at the heart of your farm. Water it with a drop of your blood every day for a week. Let it grow into a nexus point."

"And if I do that?"

"Your plants will thrive. Your vine will be satisfied. And you'll be bound to the abyss in a way no surface dweller has ever been bound before." His eyes met mine. "You'll be changed. The corruption will mark you. There's no going back."

Lysander grabbed my shoulder. "Don't do this. We can find another way. The Dawn Council—"

"The Dawn Council wants me dead." I shook him off. "The nobles want me dead. The only reason I'm still alive is because the abyss is at our doorstep and they need a scapegoat when everything falls apart."

"So you're going to give them a real monster to fear?"

I looked at my arm. The veins had reached my shoulder now, creeping toward my heart. Three days. Maybe four.

"I'm going to give them someone who can actually fight back."

---

The corruption seed was smaller than I expected. No bigger than a grain of rice, it sat in my palm, pulsing with an inner darkness that seemed to drink the light around it.

"It's beautiful," I whispered.

"Don't say that." Lysander stood at the edge of my farm, his face pale. "Don't ever say that about the abyss."

I ignored him. The past two days had taught me something important about this world: the surface wasn't good, and the abyss wasn't evil. They were just different. Different rules. Different hungers. Different ways of surviving.

My farm had grown since I first arrived. What started as a patch of dead earth was now a thriving ecosystem of abyss plants and surface herbs, their roots intertwined in ways that shouldn't have been possible. The wall I'd built was covered in blazethorn vines, their red thorns gleaming like rubies in the afternoon light.

But it wasn't enough. The herbs I harvested barely fed a dozen people. The healing salves I made could treat minor wounds. The blazethorn spears could pierce abyss creatures, but they shattered after a single use.

The city needed more. The refugees needed more. And I needed to live long enough to give it to them.

"This is insane." Lysander followed me as I walked to the center of my farm. "You're opening a door you can't close."

"Every choice I've made since arriving here has been about survival." I knelt, clearing away the soil with my bare hands. "This is no different."

"You're not just surviving anymore. You're choosing what kind of monster you become."

I paused, dirt caking my fingers. "Monsters saved me. Monsters taught me. Monsters gave me a chance when your people would have let me die in that crater."

"The abyss creatures aren't your friends. They're predators waiting for weakness."

"Maybe." I pressed the seed into the earth. "But they're honest about what they want."

A drop of blood fell from my fingertip, landing on the buried seed. The ground trembled. Roots erupted from the soil, black and grasping, wrapping around my wrist where the vine had already marked me.

"What's happening?" Lysander drew his sword.

The roots pulled. I felt myself sinking, not into the ground but into something deeper. A connection. A thread of darkness that stretched down through the earth, through the crust, through the divide between surface and abyss.

I saw the abyss. Not the tunnels and caves I'd explored, but the heart of it. A great darkness that pulsed like a living thing, vast and hungry and patient.

And it saw me.

*Little rancher,* a voice whispered in my mind. *You've finally come to bargain.*

The roots released me. I fell backward, gasping, my body covered in sweat. The mark on my arm had changed—no longer just veins of red, but a pattern of scales that shimmered like obsidian.

"Chen!" Lysander caught me before I hit the ground. "What did you do?"

I looked at my farm. The blazethorn had grown ten feet in seconds, their thorns now the size of daggers. The herbs had multiplied, covering every inch of soil in a carpet of green and purple. The wall creaked as new growth pushed against it.

But in the center of it all, a single black flower had bloomed. Its petals were the color of the void between stars, and its scent was the smell of ozone and blood.

"The connection is made." I stood, steadying myself on Lysander's shoulder. "The abyss has accepted my offering."

"At what cost?"

I looked at my reflection in a puddle of water. My eyes had changed. Where they'd been brown, they were now black from edge to edge, with flecks of silver swimming in the darkness.

"I don't know yet." I turned away from my reflection. "But I'm about to find out."

The ground shook. In the distance, alarms began to sound. Not the drills, but the real thing—the deep, resonant bells that meant the abyss was rising.

Lysander's face went white. "That's the invasion siren."

"No." I felt it in my bones, in the new connection that ran through my farm and into the depths below. "That's a response."

"To what?"

I walked to the black flower and touched a single petal. It was cold as ice and sharp as glass. "To me. I just announced my presence to every creature in the abyss. They know there's a new power on the surface."

"How many are coming?"

I closed my eyes, reaching through the connection. I could feel them—hundreds, thousands, a tide of corruption rising from the depths.

"All of them."

The first crash against the city walls sounded like thunder. Lysander grabbed my arm, pulling me toward the garrison. "We need to get to the command center."

"No." I planted my feet, feeling the roots of my farm pulse beneath me. "This is where I need to be."

"Chen—"

"They're coming for me." I looked at the black flower, at the abyss herbs, at the blazethorn that had grown into weapons. "So I'm going to give them a fight they'll remember."

The wall exploded inward, and the abyss poured through.

title: Chapter 17
content:
CHAPTER 17: THE PRICE OF GROWTH

The bloodvine shuddered.

Lin Shu felt it through the soil, through the roots that had woven themselves into his very bones over the past three weeks. The creature in the Abyss had stopped pacing. It was watching.

"Something's wrong," he muttered, wiping sweat from his brow. The morning sun hadn't fully risen over the hollow, but he'd been working since before dawn. The terraced plots he'd carved into the hillside now held seven different types of abyss-adapted crops. His great-grandfather's book lay open on a flat stone nearby, pages fluttering in the damp air.

Old Chen looked up from where he was tending the blue-veined mushrooms. "The vine?"

"Yeah." Lin Shu pressed his palm to the thickest stalk, feeling the vibrations travel up through the earth. The bloodvine had grown in the weeks since he'd planted it—grown until it wrapped around the entire hollow like a guardian serpent. Its leaves had turned dark crimson, the color of dried blood. "It's afraid."

"Vines don't feel fear," Chen gruffed, but his hands stilled on the mushrooms.

"Tell that to the vine."

A scream ripped through the morning air.

Lin Shu was already running before his mind caught up. The sound came from the eastern edge of the hollow, where Li Wei had set up her observation post. She'd been mapping the abyss creature's patrol patterns, tracking how close it came to the barrier between worlds.

He found her pressed against the cliff face, face white as chalk, pointing with a shaking finger at the ground.

The barrier was thinning.

He could see it—a shimmer in the air, like heat rising off asphalt in a Chengdu summer. Beyond it, shapes moved. Not the hulking form of the creature that had been stalking them, but smaller things. Faster things.

"Rats," Li Wei breathed. "Hundreds of them. Thousands."

Lin Shu squinted through the distortion. She was right. The ground beyond the barrier writhed with movement, a carpet of black fur and red eyes. The abyss rats had found them.

"Get everyone to the cave," he ordered, already turning back toward the farm. "Grab the red stalks, the ones with the thorns. And all the salt we've got."

Li Wei grabbed his arm. "Lin Shu, the barrier—"

"I know." He pulled free. "Just move."

The hollow erupted into controlled chaos. Mei Lin herded the children toward the cave while Old Chen and Zhang Wei loaded carts with harvested crops. Lin Shu's hands moved on autopilot, gathering the most aggressive plants, the ones that had shown they could fight back.

The bloodvine trembled against his skin.

*They're coming through.*

The thought wasn't words, exactly. More like a pressure in his skull, a knowing that didn't belong to him. The vine was communicating. Growing more intelligent by the day.

"How long?" he asked aloud.

*Hours. Maybe less. The creature in the deep is pushing them.*

Lin Shu's blood ran cold. The creature wasn't just pacing anymore. It was herding.

By midday, the hollow had become a fortress.

Lin Shu stood atop the largest terrace, looking down at the shimmering wall that separated their world from the abyss. The rats had stopped moving. They sat in perfect rows, watching, waiting.

"They're organized," Mei Lin said, handing him a cup of water. Her hands were steady now, but dark circles ringed her eyes. None of them had slept well in days.

"Too organized." He drained the cup in one gulp. "This isn't natural. Something's directing them."

"The creature?"

"Maybe." But even as he said it, Lin Shu felt the truth in his bones. The creature was powerful, yes. But this level of coordination felt different. Colder. More calculating.

A flicker of movement caught his eye. One of the rats had separated from the pack. It walked forward slowly, deliberately, until it stood at the very edge of the barrier.

Then it spoke.

"*Farmer.*"

The word came out wrong—layered, as if multiple voices spoke at once. The rat's mouth moved, but the sound seemed to come from everywhere at once.

Lin Shu felt the hollow go silent behind him. Everyone had heard it.

"Who's asking?" He kept his voice steady, though his heart hammered against his ribs.

"*I am the one who watches. The one who hungers. The one who has waited.*" The rat tilted its head, red eyes fixed on Lin Shu. "*You have been busy, little farmer. Growing things that should not grow. Bridging worlds that should remain separate.*"

"I'm just trying to survive."

"*Survival is a lie you tell yourself. You are changing the balance. The abyss feels your presence. It adapts to your crops. Your bloodvine grows stronger every day, and with each growth, it calls to deeper things.*"

Lin Shu's hand went to the vine wrapped around his wrist. It pulsed warmly against his skin, protective. "What do you want?"

"*I want to offer you a choice.*" The rat's voice shifted, becoming almost reasonable. Almost human. "*Leave this place. Take your people back to the surface. Forget what you have learned here. The abyss will seal itself, and you will be free.*"

"Free to starve? Free to let people die of the sickness?"

"*Free to live out your natural span in ignorance. It is a gift, little farmer. One I do not offer lightly.*"

Lin Shu looked at the people behind him. At Mei Lin, who had lost her entire family to the abyss sickness. At Old Chen, whose lungs were half-rotted from the spores. At the children who had been born into this dying world.

"And if I refuse?"

The rat's mouth stretched into something that might have been a smile. "*Then your hollow will become a battlefield. Your crops will be devoured. Your people will be consumed. And you will watch it all before the abyss takes you last.*"

"Is that your only threat? Eating my plants?"

"*No.*" The rat's eyes glowed brighter. "*That is merely the first wave. The abyss has deeper hungers than you know. Creatures that do not eat crops. Creatures that eat farmers.*"

The bloodvine tightened around Lin Shu's arm, and he felt a surge of understanding. The vine was trying to tell him something. Showing him images. A creature in the deep, yes, but it wasn't the one speaking through the rat. The real threat was older. Smarter. Buried so deep in the abyss that even the other monsters feared it.

"What are you?" he whispered.

The rat's form began to dissolve, melting into shadow. "*I am what waits at the bottom of all things. I am the hunger that cannot be sated. And I am patient, little farmer. More patient than you can imagine.*"

The barrier shimmered and the rat was gone, dissolving back into the swarm. The watching horde surged forward, pressing against the barrier, and Lin Shu saw what the creature had been doing while it spoke.

The rats had been digging.

Tunnels. Hundreds of them. Burrowing under the foundation of the barrier, through the earth that separated their world from the abyss.

"Everyone inside the cave!" he shouted. "Now!"

They ran. Grabbed what they could. Abandoned the terraces they had worked so hard to build. Lin Shu was the last to retreat, standing at the cave entrance as the first rat pushed through the barrier's edge.

The bloodvine roared.

It erupted from the ground in a wall of thorns and crimson leaves, slamming into the first wave of rats. Bodies flew. The vine wrapped around the tunnel entrances, sealing them shut. But even as Lin Shu watched, he saw more tunnels opening. The rats were everywhere, digging through the soil, through the rock, through anything that wasn't reinforced.

"You can't hold them all," Mei Lin said, appearing at his side.

"Watch me."

He reached deep into the connection he shared with the vine, pouring his will into it. The plant responded, growing thicker, denser. It wrapped around the entire hollow, creating a dome of living plant matter.

But he could feel it straining. The vine was strong, but it was still young. Still growing. And the rats kept coming.

A scream from inside the cave.

Lin Shu turned to see a rat dropping from the ceiling, landing on one of the children. Mei Lin moved faster than he thought possible, grabbing the creature and throwing it against the wall. It shattered on impact, but more were coming. They had found a crack in the mountain. A natural fissure that led straight into the cave system.

"We're trapped," Old Chen said, his voice hollow.

"No." Lin Shu's mind raced. The book. His great-grandfather's book. There had been a passage, something he'd skimmed over. A technique for accelerating growth. For forcing a plant to mature in minutes instead of months.

The price had been too high. He'd dismissed it as impossible.

Now he had no choice.

"Everyone to the back of the cave," he ordered. "Cover your ears. Don't open your eyes until I say."

"What are you going to do?" Mei Lin asked, her face pale.

"Something stupid." He pulled out his knife and sliced open his palm. The blood dripped onto the cave floor, and he felt the vine respond. It wanted this. Had been waiting for this.

He began to chant.

The words came from the book, but they felt older. Deeper. Like they had been waiting in his bones for generations. The bloodvine grew, faster and faster, wrapping around him, through him, becoming part of him. He felt his consciousness expand, spreading through the roots, feeling every rat in every tunnel, sensing the vast network of the abyss beyond.

And then he felt something else.

Something vast. Ancient. Hungry.

It turned its attention toward him, and Lin Shu understood what the creature had meant.

The abyss was not a place. It was a thing. A living, thinking entity that had been waiting for centuries for someone stupid enough to open a door.

And he had just painted a target on his back.

The vine pulsed once, twice, and then exploded outward, filling every tunnel, every crack, every space where a rat could hide. The screams of the creatures filled his mind, thousands of tiny deaths, and with each one he felt himself changing.

The price of accelerated growth was not just blood.

It was a piece of his soul.

When it was over, Lin Shu collapsed to his knees. The cave was silent. The rats were gone. But he could still feel them, trapped in the vine's embrace, their life force flowing into him.

And in the distance, at the very edge of his awareness, he heard laughter.

The creature in the abyss was amused.

"You've grown," it whispered, the voice echoing through the roots. "But you've also made yourself visible. And now, little farmer, everyone knows where you are."

Lin Shu looked at his hands. They were covered in blood, some of it his, most of it not. The bloodvine had retreated into his skin, forming patterns like veins, like roots, like the script in his great-grandfather's book.

He had become part of the abyss.

And the abyss had become part of him.

"We need to move," he said, his voice hoarse. "We need to leave this hollow. Now."

Mei Lin stared at him, her eyes wide. "Where?"

Lin Shu looked up at the cave ceiling, at the rock that separated them from the surface world. "Up. We need to go up. Before the abyss decides to come down."

But even as he said it

title: Chapter 18
content:
CHAPTER 18: The Roots That Bind

The bloodvine trembled against my palm like a living thing afraid.

Three days had passed since the Tribunal's visit. Three days of waiting for their promised judgment. Three days of watching the crystal deposits on the cliff face pulse with an angry red light I'd never seen before.

I knelt in the main garden, my hands buried in the soil. The vine's roots had spread faster than I expected—tendrils now reached into the abyssal earth beneath the farm, threading through the barrier between worlds like needles through silk.

"Still sulking?" Mei's voice carried across the field.

"I'm thinking."

"You've been thinking for three days. The goats are getting restless."

I looked up. She stood at the garden's edge, arms crossed, a fresh bruise blooming on her jaw. The merchants who'd fled the attack had returned with reinforcements, demanding answers about the corruption spreading through the lower districts.

"The goats can wait."

"They can't." She walked closer, her boots leaving deep prints in the dark soil. "The Tribunal's coming tomorrow. You need to decide what you're going to tell them."

"I'll tell them the truth."

"The truth?" She laughed, but there was no humor in it. "The truth is you're the first human to farm abyssal soil. The truth is your blood can purify corruption. The truth is you have no idea why any of this works."

I pulled my hands from the earth. The vine's leaves curled inward, as if ashamed.

"I know what I saw in the Abyss. The infection there—it's not natural. Something's pushing it."

"And you think the Tribunal will care?"

"They should. That infection killed thirty people last month. It's only getting worse."

Mei crouched beside me, her voice dropping low. "The Tribunal doesn't care about dead merchants. They care about power. About control. You're growing crops that shouldn't exist in soil that should be toxic. That makes you either a miracle or a threat."

"Which one am I?"

"I don't know yet. But I'd guess they don't either. That's what scares me."

She stood, brushing dirt from her knees. "There's something else. A messenger came while you were working. From the southern frontier."

I frowned. "What does the frontier want with me?"

"He didn't say. Just that it's urgent and you're to meet him at the Wandering Dog tavern tonight."

The Wandering Dog. A hole-in-the-wall establishment in the worst part of the merchant district, where deals were made in shadows and debts were paid in blood.

"Why there?"

"Because whoever sent the message doesn't want the Tribunal knowing about it." She tossed me a cloth-wrapped bundle. "I brought you a change of clothes. You'll stand out less in the dark."

I caught the bundle, feeling the rough fabric beneath my fingers. "You're not coming?"

"Someone needs to watch the farm. And if you don't come back, I need to know who to kill."

---

The Wandering Dog lived up to its reputation.

The tavern sat at the end of a narrow alley, its wooden sign hanging crooked from a single rusted chain. The windows were covered in grime so thick it looked like paint. Inside, the air was thick with smoke and the smell of cheap ale and desperation.

I found the messenger in a corner booth, his face hidden beneath a hood despite the dim lighting.

"You're late," he said, his voice rough as gravel.

"Traffic." I slid into the seat across from him. "Who sent you?"

"Someone who knows what you're carrying in that soil of yours." He pushed a folded piece of paper across the table. "Read it. Then burn it."

I unfolded the paper. The handwriting was cramped, hurried, as if written in great haste.

*Rancher,*

*You don't know me, but I know you. I know about the vine. I know about the crystals. And I know what happened in the Abyss three weeks ago.*

*The Tribunal will claim you're a threat. They'll try to take your farm, your seeds, your blood. They'll say it's for the good of the city.*

*Don't believe them.*

*The corruption isn't spreading because of you. It's spreading because of them. They've been digging into the Abyss for years, looking for something. They found it. And now they're trying to cover it up.*

*If you want proof, come to the old quarry at midnight. Alone. Bring your vine.*

*Come alone, or don't come at all.*

*—A friend*

I read the letter twice, my heart hammering against my ribs.

"You know what this says?" I asked the messenger.

"I don't read other people's mail. I just deliver it."

"What's your name?"

"Doesn't matter. But if you're smart, you'll stay away from that quarry."

"Why?"

He leaned forward, and for the first time, I saw his face. A long scar ran from his forehead to his jaw, pulling his mouth into a permanent sneer. "Because the last person who went there to meet that 'friend' ended up with their throat cut. And their body was never found."

He stood, tossing a few coins on the table. "Consider this a friendly warning. The Abyss takes care of its own."

He disappeared into the crowd before I could ask another question.

---

I didn't go to the quarry that night.

I went back to the farm, sat in the garden, and waited for dawn. Mei found me there, the vine wrapped around my arm like a serpent.

"You didn't go."

"No."

"Why not?"

"Because it was too convenient." I held up the letter. "Someone wants me to walk into a trap with my only weapon. That's not a friend. That's a hunter."

"Or someone desperate."

"Desperate people don't leave anonymous letters. They show up at your door and beg."

Mei sat beside me, her shoulder brushing mine. "So what now?"

"The Tribunal comes tomorrow. I need to know what I'm walking into."

"And the letter?"

"Real. Probably. But not from a friend." I looked at the vine, its leaves shimmering in the pale dawn light. "Someone's trying to play both sides. And I need to figure out who before it's too late."

---

The Tribunal arrived at noon.

Three of them, just like before. But this time, they didn't wait at the gate. They walked straight through the garden, their boots crushing seedlings that had taken me days to plant.

"Rancher." First Speaker Lin's voice was flat, emotionless. "We've reached our decision."

I stood in the center of the garden, Mei at my side. The bloodvine coiled at my feet, ready.

"Then say it."

"Your farm will be placed under Imperial supervision. All crops, seeds, and cultivation methods will be documented and regulated. You will be required to provide regular reports on your progress and submit to monthly inspections."

"And if I refuse?"

Lin's eyes narrowed. "Then your farm will be seized. You'll be arrested for possession of illegal cultivation materials and conspiracy with abyssal entities."

"Conspiracy?" I laughed. "I'm growing vegetables."

"You're growing weapons." She gestured to the vine. "That creature is not natural. It's not from this world. And you've bound it to your blood."

"I saved it. It saved me. That's not conspiracy. That's survival."

"Semantics."

I felt the vine stir beneath my feet, its energy pulsing through the soil. "You're afraid of me."

"We're cautious. There's a difference."

"No. You're afraid." I stepped closer, and I saw the guards behind her tense. "You know what I saw in the Abyss. You know what's happening there. And you're trying to control the only person who can stop it."

Lin's face remained impassive, but I caught a flicker of something in her eyes. Fear.

"You're making a mistake, Rancher."

"Maybe. But I've been making mistakes since the day I fell into this world. At least this one's my own."

I turned my back on her, walking toward the farmhouse. "You want to supervise? Fine. But you don't touch my crops. You don't touch my vine. And you don't step foot in my garden without my permission."

"You can't—"

"I can. Watch me."

I didn't look back. I could feel her stare burning into my back, but I kept walking. Mei followed, her hand resting on the knife at her belt.

"You just made an enemy," she whispered.

"I know."

"Are you sure that's smart?"

"No." I opened the farmhouse door, stepping inside. "But I'm running out of options."

---

That night, I lay awake, staring at the ceiling.

The vine had coiled itself around my bedpost, its leaves catching the moonlight through the window. It pulsed with a soft, rhythmic glow, like a heartbeat.

I reached out, touching one of the leaves. It curled around my finger, warm and alive.

"What are you?" I whispered.

The vine didn't answer. But somewhere deep in the soil beneath the farm, I felt something stir. Something old. Something hungry.

And for the first time since I'd arrived in this world, I wasn't sure if the vine was my ally or my leash.

The farmhouse creaked.

I sat up, reaching for the knife under my pillow.

The door swung open.

And a figure stood in the doorway, silhouetted against the moonlight.

"Didn't your mother teach you to knock?" I said, my voice steady.

The figure stepped forward, and I saw her face.

It was the messenger from the Wandering Dog. But different. Younger. Softer. The scar on her face was gone, replaced by smooth, pale skin.

"I lied about the letter," she said. "It was from me."

"Who are you?"

"My name is Lian. And I'm the only person who can tell you the truth about what's growing in your garden."

She held out her hand. In her palm, a seed glowed with the same crimson light as my vine.

"Come with me. Or stay and let them take everything you've built."

I looked at the seed. At her face. At the vine coiled around my bedpost, watching.

Then I looked past her, out the window, at the farm I'd built from nothing.

"Where are we going?"

"To the source." She smiled, and there was something ancient in her eyes. "To the heart of the Abyss."

The vine tightened around my arm. Not in warning. In agreement.

And I knew—whatever I chose, there was no going back.

title: Chapter 19
content:
CHAPTER 19: The Roots Beneath the City

The morning light hit the Abyss Gate like it always did—pale and uncertain, as if the sun itself wasn't sure it wanted to touch that shimmering membrane. I stood at the edge of Field Seven, watching my void vines pulse with that sickly purple glow they'd developed overnight.

Something was wrong.

Not just the glow. The soil beneath my feet hummed. A low, constant vibration that I'd initially written off as my imagination. But it had been three days now, and my imagination didn't leave boot prints in the dirt.

"Boss." Xiao Mei's voice cut through my thoughts. She was crouched at the far end of the field, her fingers buried in the earth. "You need to see this."

I crossed the distance in ten quick strides. The moment I knelt beside her, I felt it—heat. Real, actual heat rising from the ground. The soil here was dry, cracked, and when I pressed my palm flat against it, I jerked back.

"Shit."

"It's getting worse," Xiao Mei said. She pulled her hand out and showed me her fingers. They were covered in black residue, like ash mixed with oil. "This came from three inches down."

I'd seen that substance before. In my great-grandfather's journal, page forty-seven, under a section titled *Corruption of the Soil from Within*. The illustration showed roots—not plant roots, but something else, something that looked disturbingly like veins—pushing up through the ground, carrying darkness from the abyss into the surface world.

I'd thought it was a metaphor.

"I need you to dig a trench around the entire perimeter," I said, standing up. "Three feet deep. If you find anything that looks like roots, don't touch it. Call me."

Xiao Mei nodded and ran off to gather the other ranchers. I watched her go, then turned back to the Gate.

The membrane was different today. The usual shimmer had become a constant ripple, like water about to boil. And the colors—I'd never seen those colors before. Deep purples and angry reds swirled together, forming patterns that made my eyes water when I tried to focus on them.

"Beautiful, isn't it?"

I didn't turn around. I knew that voice.

"Chen Wei." I kept my eyes on the Gate. "I thought I told you to stay out of my fields."

"You did." The city lord's footsteps crunched across the dirt. "But I received a rather concerning report about unusual energy readings coming from this location. As acting authority of the surface district, I have a responsibility to investigate."

I finally turned to face him. He was dressed in his usual pristine white robes, a stark contrast to my dirt-caked work clothes. Behind him stood four guards in full combat gear, their hands resting on the hilts of their void-steel blades.

"Energy readings?"

"Don't play dumb, farmer." Chen Wei's smile didn't reach his eyes. "The monitoring array at the city center picked up a spike three nights ago. Right around the time your precious vines started glowing. Care to explain?"

Three nights ago. That was when I'd performed the first harvest of the void vines. When I'd cut into their stems and watched black liquid drip onto the soil, only to have it absorbed instantly. When the vines had grown back twice as thick by morning.

"I've been experimenting with new cultivation methods," I said carefully. "Nothing that concerns the city."

"Everything that happens on this surface concerns the city." Chen Wei stepped closer. "You think I don't know what you're doing? You think I can't see what you're growing here?"

He gestured at the fields. At the void vines that now covered every inch of arable land, their purple glow casting strange shadows in the daylight. At the abyss goats that had grown to twice their normal size, their eyes now completely black. At the soil that steamed in patches, releasing wisps of dark vapor.

"I see the abyss in your crops," Chen Wei continued. "I see it in your animals. And I see it in you."

I felt my jaw tighten. "The abyss is a tool. Like fire. Like steel. It can be used for good or ill."

"And who decides which is which?" He was close enough now that I could smell the incense on his robes. "You? A delivery driver from a world that couldn't even handle its own pollution? You think you understand forces that have existed since before humanity crawled out of caves?"

"I understand that starving people need food."

"People starving because your methods are destabilizing the entire ecosystem!" He grabbed my collar, and I let him. "The underground rivers are turning black. The wildlife is migrating toward the abyss. The very soil is—"

"Let go of me."

The words came out cold. Flat. I felt something stir in my chest, that familiar pressure that had been building since the day I'd first planted seeds in this cursed ground.

Chen Wei's eyes widened, but he didn't let go. "What did you—"

The ground exploded between us.

Not a real explosion—more like a violent upheaval. Soil and rocks flew in all directions as something burst from beneath our feet. I stumbled back, pulling Chen Wei with me, and we both hit the ground hard.

When the dust cleared, I saw it.

A root. No, not a root. A tendril. Thick as my arm and black as the void between stars. It rose from the hole in the earth like a serpent, swaying gently, as if tasting the air.

Then it turned toward me.

I scrambled to my feet, my heart pounding. The tendril moved with impossible speed, wrapping around my ankle before I could run. I felt its grip tighten, felt it start to pull me toward the hole.

"Cut it!" I shouted. "Someone cut it!"

But the guards were frozen, their weapons half-drawn, their faces pale. Even Chen Wei was staring in horror.

The tendril pulled harder. I dug my heels into the dirt, but it was no use. I was being dragged across the field, past my glowing vines, past the screaming ranchers, straight toward the hole that had opened in the earth.

And then I saw what was inside.

The hole wasn't just a hole. It was a tunnel. A tunnel lined with more of those black tendrils, all of them pulsing with the same sickly purple light as my vines. And at the bottom, far below, I could see something moving. Something massive.

Something that had been waiting.

"Your father was right about you."

The voice came from everywhere and nowhere. It was ancient, cold, and utterly without emotion.

"He planted the seeds, but you watered them. You fed them. You gave them purpose."

The tendril tightened around my leg, and I felt my bones grind together.

"Come home, grandson. The roots have been waiting."

I was pulled into the darkness.

The last thing I saw was Chen Wei's face, his mouth open in a silent scream. Then the earth closed over my head, and I was falling, falling, falling into a world of roots and shadows and the whispered promises of a man I'd never met.

I hit something hard and wet. Mud, I realized. I was lying in mud that smelled of iron and decay. Above me, the tunnel entrance was already sealing, the tendrils weaving together to form a solid roof of darkness.

I was trapped.

No. Not trapped. Brought here. Deliberately.

I forced myself to stand, my legs shaking. The tunnel stretched in both directions, endless and dark. But I could feel it now—that familiar pull. The same pull that had guided me to the Abyss Gate on my first day. The same pull that had shown me where to plant my seeds.

It was coming from deeper in the tunnel.

I started walking.

The roots lined the walls, the ceiling, the floor. They pulsed with light, and as I walked, they seemed to lean toward me, as if recognizing me. As if welcoming me home.

After what felt like hours, I reached a chamber.

It was massive. Cathedral-like. The roots had formed pillars and arches, creating a space that was both beautiful and terrible. In the center of the chamber stood a figure.

He was old. Ancient. His skin was the color of bark, and his eyes were the same purple as my vines. He wore robes made of woven roots, and in his hands, he held a staff that looked disturbingly like a human spine.

"You look like him," the figure said. "Like my son. Like your father."

I stopped at the edge of the chamber. "You're dead. The journal said you died in the abyss."

"Did it?" The old man smiled, and his teeth were black. "Or did it say I was lost?"

I had no answer. The journal had been vague about my great-grandfather's fate. It had said he'd descended into the abyss and never returned. I'd assumed that meant death.

I'd assumed wrong.

"You summoned me here," I said. "Why?"

"Because I need your help." The old man—my great-grandfather—stepped forward. "The abyss is waking, grandson. And only someone who carries my blood can stop what's coming."

"Stop what? What's coming?"

He raised his staff, and the chamber trembled. The roots pulsed brighter, and I heard it—a sound like a heartbeat, coming from somewhere deep beneath us.

"The surface," he said. "The abyss is coming to the surface. Not through Gates. Not through rifts. It's going to swallow everything."

I stared at him. "Then why are you down here? Why aren't you helping?"
