"Because I'm the one who started it."

The words hit me like a physical blow. I stumbled back, my hand instinctively going to the void vine cutting knife at my belt.

"What?"

"I was young. Arrogant." His voice was hollow. "I thought I could control the abyss. I thought I could make it serve humanity. But I was wrong. The abyss doesn't serve. It consumes. And I fed it more than I ever took."

He raised his hands, and I saw them clearly for the first time. They weren't hands. They were root structures, twisted and gnarled, ending in fingers that looked like claws.

"I've been down here for sixty years," he said. "Keeping it contained. But I'm failing. The abyss is growing stronger, and my body is almost gone. I need someone to take my place."

I felt the floor shift beneath me. The roots were moving, curling around my ankles, my wrists, my waist.

"Wait—"

"I'm sorry, grandson. But you're the only one who can hold it back."

The roots tightened. I felt them pierce my skin, felt them burrow into my flesh. And in my mind, I heard a voice that was not my great-grandfather's.

*Soon.*

The abyss was coming. And I was going to be its cage.

title: The Price of Sunlight
content:
The morning sun cast long shadows across the terraced fields when Li Wei noticed something wrong with his blood-vine.

He'd been inspecting the second row of crops, counting the new buds that had sprouted overnight. Twenty-three. A good harvest, considering the abyss worms had tried to burrow into the roots again last night. But when he crouched to check the soil moisture, he saw it.

The vine's leaves had turned silver.

Not the pale gray of morning frost. Not the dusty white of dried earth. Pure, metallic silver that caught the light and threw it back like shattered mirrors.

"Old Zhang," he called, not taking his eyes off the leaves. "Come look at this."

The old farmer shuffled over, his walking stick tapping a steady rhythm. He'd been with Li Wei for three weeks now, ever since the second terrace had been cleared. The man's cough had improved, but his hands still trembled when he worked.

He looked at the silver leaves. His face went pale.

"You need to dig it up."

"What? No." Li Wei stepped between Old Zhang and the vine. "This thing saved my life. It's the only reason we have food."

"It's changing." Old Zhang's voice cracked. "I've seen this before. In the old stories, before the abyss swallowed the Eastern Kingdoms. Plants that drank too deep from the darkness. They always turned silver first."

Li Wei touched one of the leaves. It felt cold. Not the cool of morning, but the deep chill of stone that had never known sunlight. The vine shivered under his fingers.

"Tell me the stories."

Old Zhang sat down heavily on a rock. His eyes had that distant look Li Wei had learned to recognize—the look of a man remembering things he'd rather forget.

"The first farmers of the abyss didn't use seeds. They used blood. Their own blood, mixed with the bones of the dead. The plants grew fast, strong, beautiful." He paused. "Then they started to dream."

"Plants don't dream."

"These do." Old Zhang pointed at the silver leaves. "They dream of the darkness they came from. And when they dream too long, they remember. They want to go home."

The vine pulsed. Li Wei felt it in his bones, a vibration that matched his heartbeat but slightly off, like a song played in the wrong key.

"How long before it... remembers?"

Old Zhang's silence was answer enough.

Li Wei stood up. "Then we have to make it not want to go home. We have to give it something better here."

"You can't bargain with an abyss plant. They don't think like—"

"It grew from my blood." Li Wei pulled out his knife. "That means part of it is me. And I'm not going back to the darkness."

He cut his palm. The blood dripped onto the silver leaves.

The vine screamed.

Not a sound that came through air. It screamed in Li Wei's skull, a shriek of pure agony that drove him to his knees. The terraced fields spun around him. The sky turned red. He felt roots twisting through his veins, felt leaves pushing through his skin, felt—

"Stop!" Old Zhang's voice came from very far away. "You're feeding the wrong part!"

Li Wei's vision cleared. Blood still dripped from his hand, but the vine's leaves had turned black where the drops had fallen. Black as the abyss itself.

"What did I do?"

"You gave it more of yourself. But you're human. You carry the light of the surface." Old Zhang grabbed his wrist, forced the wound against his sleeve. "The plant needs abyss blood. Monster blood. The same darkness it came from."

Li Wei remembered the abyss worms he'd killed last night. Their bodies still lay in a pile by the fence, leaking black ichor onto the ground.

He dragged himself to his feet. "Then that's what we'll feed it."

---

They spent the next three days hunting.

Not the careful harvesting of crops or the cautious tending of vines. Real hunting. Li Wei led the way into the deeper cracks of the abyss, where the darkness grew thick enough to taste. Old Zhang followed with a bag of salt and a bone knife.

The worms came first. Small ones, barely as long as Li Wei's arm, their bodies translucent in the dim light. He killed them quickly, split them open, poured their blood into a clay pot.

The vine drank eagerly. Its silver leaves turned gray, then black, then settled into a deep purple that seemed to absorb light rather than reflect it.

"It's working," Li Wei said.

"It's hungrier," Old Zhang replied.

He was right. The next night, the vine sent out tendrils to search for more blood. They found Li Wei's goats first.

He woke to screaming. Not human screaming. The high, terrified bleating of animals being pulled into darkness. He ran outside with his knife and found the vine wrapped around one of his goats, its tendrils burrowing into the animal's flesh.

Li Wei cut the goat free. It died anyway. The vine had already hollowed it out.

"You see?" Old Zhang stood in the doorway, his face gray in the moonlight. "It's not a plant anymore. It's a mouth. A hungry mouth."

"Then we feed it." Li Wei's hands were shaking. "We feed it until it's full."

"We feed it until it's full of monsters. And then what? What happens when there are no more monsters?"

Li Wei didn't have an answer.

---

The morning of the fourth day, the vine bloomed.

Flowers. Hundreds of them. Each one the size of a man's head, their petals the color of dried blood. They opened at dawn and closed at dusk, and when they opened, they released a scent that made Li Wei's head swim.

"Sweet," Old Zhang said, sniffing carefully. "Too sweet. Like rotting fruit."

"Smells like hope to me." Li Wei touched one of the petals. It was soft, warm, alive in a way the vine had never been before. "We did it. We made it bloom."

"Something's coming."

Li Wei turned. A figure was walking up the path from the main road. Tall, wrapped in a gray cloak, moving with the practiced ease of someone who knew the abyss.

Li Wei's hand went to his knife.

The figure stopped at the edge of the fields. Pushed back its hood.

It was a woman. Young, maybe thirty, with dark hair pulled back in a severe bun. Her eyes were the color of amber, and they held nothing but cold calculation.

"You're the farmer," she said. Not a question.

"Who's asking?"

"My name is Chen Mei. I represent a group of people who have been watching you. We have a proposition."

"I'm not interested."

"You should be." She stepped forward, her eyes fixed on the blooming vine. "Because in three days, the abyss is going to send something to kill you. And that thing," she pointed at the vine, "is your only chance of survival."

Li Wei felt the ground shift under his feet. The vine's roots pulsed, a steady heartbeat that matched his own. He looked at Chen Mei, at her cold amber eyes, and knew she was telling the truth.

"How do you know?"

"Because we sent it."

She smiled. It was not a kind smile.

"You see, farmer, we've been waiting for someone like you. Someone who can grow things in the abyss. Someone who can bridge the darkness and the light." Her smile widened. "Someone we can use."

Old Zhang stepped forward, his walking stick raised. "Get off this land."

"Or what? You'll feed me to the vine?" Chen Mei laughed. "It would eat me gladly. But it's not hungry for human blood. Not yet."

She turned to leave, then paused.

"Three days, farmer. Three days until the hatching. The abyss worms have been gathering their strength, growing in the deep places where you can't reach. They're not worms anymore. They're something else. Something that remembers when the abyss was young and hungry."

She walked away, her cloak trailing in the dust.

Li Wei stood in his fields, surrounded by silver leaves and blood-red flowers, and felt the vine pulse beneath his feet like a living heart.

Old Zhang came to stand beside him. "What do we do?"

"We prepare." Li Wei pulled out his great-grandfather's book. The pages were filled with diagrams, notes, warnings. He flipped to a section he'd never understood before. A section titled "The Binding of Roots."

"We give the vine a reason to stay."

"And if it doesn't work?"

Li Wei looked at the silver leaves, the purple tendrils, the flowers that bloomed like wounds. He thought of the abyss worms gathering in the darkness, growing, remembering.

"Then we burn it all down."

The vine shivered. A single petal fell from the nearest flower, landed on Li Wei's shoulder, and stuck there like a brand.

He didn't brush it off.

---

That night, Li Wei dreamed of roots.

They grew through his body, pushing through his veins, splitting his bones. His skin turned to bark. His blood turned to sap. He felt the abyss calling to him, a voice like the grinding of stones, like the crying of the dead.

*Come home,* it whispered. *Come back to the darkness.*

He woke with the vine wrapped around his wrist, its tendrils buried in his skin.

He cut himself free. The vine bled. Black ichor that smelled of rot.

Old Zhang was standing in the doorway, his face unreadable.

"It's too late," he said. "You've already become part of it."

Li Wei looked at his wrist. The vine's tendrils were still there, embedded in his flesh, pulsing with a rhythm that matched his heart.

"I know."

He stood up. The vine reached for him, its tendrils stretching, grasping. He let them touch his hand.

"Then we become something worse."

The vine's flowers opened. Their scent filled the air, sweet and rotting, hope and despair mixed together.

Somewhere in the distance, the ground trembled.

The hatching had begun.

title: Chapter 12
content:
CHAPTER 12: The Warden's Gambit

The bloodvine lashed out before I could breathe.

Not at me—at the Warden. The black-robed figure barely had time to raise his hand before the vine wrapped around his wrist, thorns digging deep. He hissed, purple light flaring from his palm, but the vine didn't burn. It tightened.

I stared at my hand. The vine had moved on its own.

"You..." the Warden growled, his voice cracking like old leather. "You've been feeding it."

I didn't answer. I was too busy trying to figure out why I wasn't dead. The last thing I remembered was falling into this pit with a pocket full of abyss-tainted soil and a head full of questions. Now I was standing in what looked like a cathedral carved from obsidian, facing a man whose face kept shifting between shapes.

The bloodvine pulsed. Warmth spread up my arm.

"The old man's blood runs strong in you," the Warden said, wrenching the vine free. Black ichor dripped from his wounds. "But blood alone won't save you here."

He gestured. The obsidian floor rippled.

I jumped back as something erupted from the stone—a mass of writhing roots, each one tipped with a glowing purple eye. They turned toward me, blinking in unison.

"What the hell are those?"

"Watchers." The Warden's face settled on something approximating human. Middle-aged, weathered, with eyes that had seen too much. "They see everything that happens in the abyss. And right now, they're seeing you."

The roots lunged.

I ran.

Not proud of it, but I'm a delivery driver, not a fighter. My instincts screamed to find cover, find an exit, find anything that wasn't a horde of eyeball-covered tentacles. I dove behind a pillar as the roots slammed into it, cracking the obsidian.

The book in my pocket burned.

I pulled it out. The pages were glowing, the symbols shifting and rearranging themselves. My great-grandfather's handwriting appeared, fresh as if written moments ago:

*The abyss fears what it cannot consume. Plant your seeds where the darkness is thinnest. Water them with your will. Harvest what grows.*

"Great, poetry," I muttered. "Very helpful."

The pillar cracked again. I rolled sideways as a root punched through, inches from my skull.

The bloodvine writhed on my arm, desperate to join the fight. I let it. It shot forward, wrapping around the root and squeezing. The eye at its tip burst, spraying black fluid.

The Warden laughed.

"First lesson, boy. You can't kill what's already dead."

He was right. The root kept moving, the burst eye reforming from the black ichor. More roots joined it, surrounding me in a circle of blinking, unblinking eyes.

I clutched the book. "What do I do?"

No answer. Just more shifting symbols.

The roots closed in.

I thought about Chengdu. About my apartment, my bike, the noodles I'd never get to eat again. About the old man's book and the vine that grew from his blood. About the soil in my pocket, still warm from the abyss.

Soil.

I reached into my pocket, grabbed a handful, and threw it at the nearest root.

The effect was immediate. The root recoiled, the eye on its tip shriveling. Where the soil touched, black cracks spread, eating away at the root like acid.

The Warden's face twisted. "What did you just do?"

"Farming," I said, and grabbed another handful.

The roots retreated, hissing. I stood in the center of the circle, soil in one hand, bloodvine in the other, the book glowing in my pocket.

"You're not supposed to have that," the Warden whispered. "The surface soil—it's been purified. That's impossible."

"I'm full of surprises."

I threw more soil. The roots fled, dissolving into the obsidian floor. The Watchers' eyes blinked out one by one, leaving us in darkness.

Then the torches relit.

The Warden stood alone, his black robes tattered, his face flickering between a dozen different people. Old, young, male, female. All of them staring at me with the same hollow eyes.

"Your great-grandfather thought he could change things," he said. "He planted his seeds. He harvested his crops. And where did it get him?"

"I don't know. Where?"

"Dead. In a ditch. His body feeding the very abyss he tried to tame."

The bloodvine pulsed. Angry. Hungry.

I looked at my arm. The vine had grown thicker, its thorns sharper. It wrapped around my forearm like a living gauntlet, pulsing in rhythm with my heartbeat.

"He's not dead," I said. "His blood is still here. In this vine. In this book. In me."

The Warden's face settled on a young woman's. Beautiful. Deadly. She smiled.

"Then let me show you what his blood truly bought."

She raised her hand. The obsidian floor split open, revealing a chasm of absolute darkness. From it rose a sound—low, rumbling, like the earth itself was groaning.

Something was climbing out.

I backed away, but the bloodvine held firm. It wrapped around my waist, anchoring me to the spot.

"What are you doing?" I shouted.

The vine tightened. Pulled me forward.

Toward the chasm.

"Your inheritance awaits," the Warden said, her voice echoing from every direction. "The harvest your great-grandfather planted. The fruits of his labor."

I dug my heels in. "I don't want it."

"Too late. The seeds were sown the moment you touched the abyss. The blood was spilled the moment you opened the book. You are his legacy, boy. Whether you like it or not."

The chasm yawned wider. Something massive shifted in the darkness below. I caught a glimpse of eyes—dozens, hundreds, thousands of eyes—all opening at once.

The bloodvine pulled.

I fell.

The darkness swallowed me whole, but I didn't hit bottom. I floated, suspended in nothing, the book's glow the only light. Its pages flipped on their own, stopping at a page I'd never seen before.

In the center was a single word, written in blood:

*Grow.*

The bloodvine exploded.

It burst from my arm, my chest, my legs—everywhere it had touched. Thorns pierced my skin, but there was no pain. Just warmth. Just light.

I was changing.

The abyss around me recoiled, but the vine held fast. It was feeding, drawing something from the darkness, turning it into... what?

I looked at my hands. They were covered in bark. No—not bark. Something like it. Living wood, pulsing with purple veins.

"I'm turning into a tree," I whispered.

The book's pages flipped again:

*The first Warden was not born. He was planted.*

I laughed. It came out broken, desperate.

"Great-grandfather, what the hell did you get me into?"

The darkness answered with a roar.

Whatever was in the chasm with me, it wasn't happy about my transformation. The eyes I'd seen earlier were closing, one by one, but not before I saw what they belonged to.

A face.

A face so vast I couldn't see its edges. A face made of abyss and hunger and ancient, patient hate.

The Warden's voice came from everywhere and nowhere:

"The abyss has a heart, boy. And you're standing in its chambers."

The eyes opened.

All of them.

I screamed.

The bloodvine didn't. It kept growing, wrapping around me in layers of bark and thorns and purple light. I felt it digging into the abyss, drawing sustenance, turning poison into power.

But the face was still there. Still watching. Still hungry.

And I was still falling.

The book burned hotter. New words appeared, written in fire:

*To defeat the abyss, become its master. To become its master, first become its soil.*

"What does that mean?" I shouted.

No answer. Just the flames, and the face, and the endless fall.

The bloodvine tightened around my chest, squeezing until I couldn't breathe. My vision blurred. The warmth turned to heat, then to something beyond pain.

I was dissolving.

Becoming part of the abyss.

And somewhere, far above, the Warden was laughing.

The last thing I saw before the darkness took me was the book's final page:

*Chapter One: How to Plant a God.*

title: The Governor's Reckoning
content:
The morning sun cast long shadows across the Abyss Rancher's plot as Li Wei examined his latest harvest. The bloodvine had grown three new tendrils overnight, each one pulsing with that sickly green light that still made his stomach turn. The corrupted soil beneath his feet had shifted from black to a deep purple, almost royal in its hue.

"Faster now," Chen Wei said, appearing at his elbow with her characteristic silence. "The growth rate has increased by forty percent since yesterday."

Li Wei didn't look up from the vine. "That's not possible. The book said three weeks minimum for the next stage."

"The book is wrong." She knelt beside him, her weathered fingers tracing the air above the glowing tendrils. "Or rather, it's incomplete. This isn't just accelerated growth—it's evolution. The vine is adapting to something."

A sharp crack echoed from the tree line. Li Wei's hand went to the ironwood staff strapped to his back, the one he'd sharpened to a razor's edge after the first night of abyssal crawler attacks. But nothing emerged from the shadows.

"Just the canopy settling," he muttered, though his eyes didn't stop scanning.

"Li Wei." Chen Wei's voice had gone flat. "When did you last check your great-grandfather's book?"

He pulled the weathered tome from his jacket pocket, the leather cover warm against his palm. The pages fell open to a section he'd memorized weeks ago—the chapter on stable growth patterns. But the words had shifted.

*When the bloodvine drinks deep of corrupted earth three times, the gate between worlds grows thin. The harvesters must be ready.*

"That's new," he whispered.

"Show me." Chen Wei took the book with reverence, her eyes scanning the ancient characters. Her face drained of color. "The harvesters. It's not referring to you."

"Then who—"

The ground shook. Not the subtle tremor of deep earth, but the violent shudder of something massive moving beneath the surface. The bloodvine's tendrils whipped outward, striking the soil like serpents hunting prey.

"What's happening?" Li Wei grabbed his staff, the wood humming against his palm.

"The gate." Chen Wei clutched the book to her chest. "Someone's opening it from the other side."

The purple soil cracked open in a perfect circle, fifty meters across. From the fissure rose a pillar of obsidian, carved with symbols that made Li Wei's skull ache just looking at them. The bloodvine wrapped around the pillar like a living cage, its tendrils digging into the black stone.

"That's not possible," Chen Wei breathed. "That's a Governor's Seal. Only the surface lords have those."

"Surface lords?"

"The nobility. The ones who rule from the safety of their walled cities while the rest of us rot in the wilds." Her voice dripped with venom. "They're supposed to be extinct. The Purge of '47 wiped out the last of their bloodlines."

The pillar hummed, and a voice emerged from the stone—not sound, but pure vibration that resonated in Li Wei's bones.

*Blood keeper. Seed planter. You have awakened the deep growth.*

Li Wei's great-grandfather's book burst into flames in Chen Wei's hands. She dropped it with a cry, but the fire didn't spread. It consumed the pages in perfect silence, leaving only ash that swirled upward in a spiral.

"No!" Li Wei dove for the remains, but the ash slipped through his fingers like water.

*The old knowledge must burn for the new to grow. You are no longer a student, Li Wei. You are the first farmer of the abyss.*

The voice was coming from inside his own skull now. Chen Wei stared at him, her eyes wide with something he'd never seen in them before: fear.

"Li Wei, don't listen to it. That's a Governor's trick—they speak in truths that become chains."

But the voice continued, relentless.

*The harvesters come at midnight. Three hundred souls from the abyss, hungry for the surface. You have until the bloodvine blooms to decide their fate.*

"You can't," Chen Wei grabbed his arm. "If you open a full gate, the surface world will be overrun. The abyss has been waiting for a foothold since the first Rift War."

"I don't have a choice." Li Wei gestured at the pillar, the glowing vine, the cracked earth. "It's already happening."

"Then we destroy it." She pulled a knife from her belt, the blade etched with symbols that matched the ones on the pillar. "I've spent forty years learning to kill abyssal growth. This is no different."

"Stop." Li Wei's voice carried a weight that surprised even himself. The vine pulsed in response, and Chen Wei froze mid-stride. "If you destroy the pillar, what happens to the souls on the other side?"

"They're abyssal. They don't have souls."

"That's not what the book said." He picked up the ash, letting it run through his fingers. "It said the harvesters need guidance. That they're lost."

"Li Wei—"

A crash from the tree line. This time, something emerged. A figure, human-shaped but wrong—too tall, too thin, its skin the color of dried blood. It wore rags that might have been fine silk centuries ago, and its eyes burned with green fire.

"Keeper," it rasped, dropping to one knee. "The first wave has breached the upper caves. We await your command."

Chen Wei raised her knife, but Li Wei put himself between her and the creature.

"Who are you?" he asked.

The creature's head tilted at an impossible angle. "I am Echo, once speaker for House Vex, now nothing but memory given form. The abyss holds many such as me—shadows of what was, waiting for purpose."

"You're a harvester."

"We are what you make us. The gate requires a key, Keeper. Your blood, your will, your choice." Echo's burning eyes fixed on him. "Three hundred souls, trapped between worlds. You can save them, or you can damn them. But you cannot leave them as they are."

Li Wei looked at Chen Wei. Her face was a mask of conflict—decades of hatred warring with something that might have been hope.

"How do I know you're telling the truth?" he asked.

Echo reached into its own chest. The claws sank through flesh like water, and when they emerged, they held a sphere of pulsing light. Inside it, Li Wei saw faces—human faces, twisted in agony, reaching for something they couldn't touch.

"These are the harvesters. Not monsters. Prisoners." Echo's voice cracked. "I was one of them, until the bloodvine called me forth. The abyss doesn't corrupt souls, Keeper. It traps them."

Chen Wei's knife clattered to the ground. "The Purge. They said the surface lords were monsters, that they had to be exterminated. But what if..." Her voice trailed off.

"What if they were just people who got trapped?" Li Wei finished.

The bloodvine bloomed. A single flower, white as bone, opened at the top of the pillar. Its petals unfurled, and from their center rose a seed the size of a man's fist, pulsing with that sickly green light.

"Midnight," Echo said. "The choice is yours."

The creature dissolved into shadow, leaving Li Wei alone with Chen Wei and the impossible weight of three hundred lives.

"We need to find the Governor," Chen Wei said quietly. "The real one, not the legends. If there's a way to control the gate without killing everyone..."

"There isn't." Li Wei picked up the seed. It was warm in his hands, humming with life. "The book burned for a reason. This was always the plan."

"Your great-grandfather couldn't have known—"

"He knew." Li Wei remembered the old man's final words, delivered through fevered lips in a hospital bed fifty years ago and a world away. *When the vine blooms, don't hesitate. The harvest waits for no one.*

"Then what do we do?" Chen Wei asked.

Li Wei looked at the pillar, the vine, the seed. Three hundred souls, trapped between worlds. A gate that could bring salvation or destruction. And a voice in his head that whispered of harvests and keepers and choices that couldn't be unmade.

"We farm," he said. "We grow the gate wide enough to let them through, and we figure out how to keep the abyss from following."

"That's insane."

"Probably." He tucked the seed into his pocket, feeling its pulse sync with his own heartbeat. "But I didn't get dragged to another dimension to play it safe."

The sun climbed higher, burning away the morning mist. In the distance, Li Wei could hear the first sounds of the surface world stirring—birds, wind, the distant clang of a blacksmith's hammer. Normal sounds for a world that had no idea what was growing beneath their feet.

By midnight, everything would change.

He just hoped he was ready for what came after.

title: Chapter 14
content:
CHAPTER 14: THE HARVEST THAT SCREAMED

Three days.

Seventy-two hours of watching the bloody vine creep across the stone like it owned the place.

Li Wei sat on his cot, the abyss-light casting his face in sickly green shadows. The vine had grown. Not just length—it had *thickened*. What started as a thin tendril was now the width of his forearm, its surface pulsing with veins that glowed faintly red.

And it had started doing something new.

Every hour, on the hour, it released a single drop of golden liquid from its tip. The drop would hang suspended for exactly three seconds before falling onto the stone below, where it sizzled and evaporated into nothing.

He'd collected three drops total in a chipped bowl. The liquid smelled like nothing he'd ever encountered—not sweet, not sour, not metallic. Just *potential*. Like the scent of rain on hot concrete back on Earth.

The book was useless on this. His great-grandfather had written extensively about planting, about soil preparation, about the proper prayers to offer before seeding. But there was nothing about a vine that grew from a man's blood and dripped liquid gold.

"Okay," Li Wei said to the empty cavern. "Let's think like a farmer."

He pulled out the book, flipped to a section he'd memorized but not understood. *The First Principle of Abyssal Agriculture: That which is grown in darkness must be harvested in light.*

But there was no light here. Not real light. The bioluminescent crystals embedded in the cavern walls gave off a pale green glow, but it wasn't sunlight. It wasn't even firelight.

Unless...

He looked at the golden drops in the bowl. They caught the green light and reflected it back as something warmer, something almost yellow.

*Harvested in light.*

Li Wei grabbed the bowl, walked to the vine. The tendril sensed his approach—he was sure of it now. It curled slightly toward him, like a cat acknowledging its owner.

"Here goes nothing."

He took a single drop on his fingertip and touched it to the vine's base.

The reaction was instant.

The vine *screamed*.

Not sound—not exactly. It was a vibration that ran through the stone floor, up his legs, into his skull. A frequency that bypassed his ears and drilled directly into his brain. Images flashed: darkness, pressure, roots digging through rock, something vast and ancient sleeping beneath the abyss.

Then the vine exploded into growth.

In ten seconds, it went from arm-thick to tree-trunk. It shot upward, punched through the cavern ceiling, kept going. Stone shattered. Dust billowed. Li Wei threw himself backward, covered his face.

When he looked up, the vine had become a pillar.

It rose through the broken ceiling into some upper cavern, its surface now covered in thorns the size of his fingers, each thorn dripping that same golden liquid. The drops fell like rain now, pattering onto the stone floor in a steady drizzle.

The abyss-light caught them and turned the cavern gold.

Li Wei's hands were shaking. Not from fear—from *hunger*. The smell hit him now, full force. It was the smell of soil after spring rain, of fresh-cut wheat, of tomatoes still warm from the sun. It was the smell of *life*.

His stomach cramped.

He hadn't eaten in days. The rations in the bag were gone, consumed in desperate mouthfuls during his first day in the abyss. He'd been running on adrenaline and terror since.

The golden droplets pooled on the floor, forming a shallow puddle that steamed gently.

*Don't be stupid*, he told himself. *You don't know what that is. It could be poison. It could be acid. It could—*

His hand moved before his brain caught up.

He dipped two fingers into the puddle, brought them to his mouth.

The taste hit him like a freight train.

It wasn't sweet. It wasn't savory. It was *completeness*. His body absorbed it instantly, and he felt strength flood back into his limbs. The hunger vanished. The fatigue lifted. Even the scratches on his arms seemed to tingle with healing.

"Holy shit," he whispered.

The vine pulsed in response. Its thorns extended, retracted, like it was breathing.

Li Wei looked at the hole in the ceiling. Through the gap, he could see another cavern above—larger, with different crystal formations. And something else. Movement. Shadows shifting in the green gloom.

*The abyss has levels.*

The thought hit him cold. He'd been in this cavern for three days, thinking it was the whole of his prison. But the abyss was deeper than he'd imagined. And apparently, higher too.

The vine had punched through to the next level. Maybe it was trying to reach the surface. Maybe it was trying to escape, just like him.

"Alright," he said, steadying his voice. "Time to see what's up there."

He grabbed his bag—empty now, but still useful—and approached the vine. Its surface was rough, the thorns sharp but manageable. He tested his weight on a thorn. It held.

Hand over hand, he climbed.

The ascent took longer than expected. The vine was slick with golden liquid, and his grip kept slipping. But the strength from the droplets burned in his muscles, pushing him upward.

He reached the ceiling, pulled himself through the gap, and emerged into a nightmare.

The cavern above was vast. Cathedral-vast. Its ceiling disappeared into darkness so complete it seemed solid. Its walls were lined with crystals that pulsed in slow rhythm, like a heartbeat.

And it was full of *nests*.

Huge structures of woven bone and dried sinew hung from every surface. Each nest was the size of a car, and each one held eggs. Dozens of eggs, each as tall as a man, their shells translucent and pulsing with inner light.

Inside the eggs, things moved.

Li Wei's hand slipped. He caught himself, heart hammering. The golden droplets on his skin seemed to glow brighter in this cavern, and he realized with dawning horror that the eggs were turning toward him.

*They can sense it. They can sense the life in the droplets.*

A crack split the nearest egg.

Then another.

Then a hundred.

The shells shattered in sequence, like dominoes falling. And from each egg emerged creatures that shouldn't exist—humanoid but not human, with too many joints and faces that shifted between expressions like water. Their skin was the color of abyss-light, green and sickly. Their eyes were solid black.

They didn't have mouths. Instead, their faces were smooth from forehead to chin, except for a single vertical slit in the center.

The slit opened.

They *screamed*.

Not sound—the same vibration as before, but amplified a thousand times. Li Wei's vision went white. His grip failed. He fell.

The vine caught him.

It wrapped around his waist, held him suspended three feet from the ground. The thorns pierced his clothes but not his skin. He hung there, swinging, as the mouthless things poured from their nests and gathered below him.

They looked up with those black eyes.

And they *bowed*.

Every single one of them. Hundreds of creatures, bowing to him like he was their king. Their slits opened and closed, opened and closed, and from them came a sound like wind through hollow bones.

*Welcome.*

The word wasn't spoken. It was *thought*—pushed directly into his brain like a hot needle.

*Welcome, Father. Welcome home.*

The vine lowered him gently to the ground. The creatures parted, creating a path that led deeper into the cavern. At its end, something waited. Something that made the other creatures look like children's drawings.

It was humanoid. Mostly. Taller than any man, with skin that shifted between colors—green, gold, black, back to green. Its face had a mouth. A real one. And it was smiling.

"Hello, little farmer," it said. Voice like gravel and honey. "We've been waiting for you. For a very long time."

Li Wei's hand went to his pocket. The book was still there. He could feel its weight, its familiar shape. But for the first time, it felt like a dead thing in his hands. Useless.

"You're the one who planted the seed," the creature continued, walking toward him. Each step left footprints that glowed gold and faded. "You're the one who watered it with your blood. You're the one who fed it."

"I don't understand," Li Wei said. His voice cracked. "I just wanted to survive."

"Survival is the first harvest." The creature stopped three feet away, looked down at him with eyes that held galaxies. "But it's not the last. You've opened a door, little farmer. A door between worlds. And now..."

It reached out, touched his chest with one long finger.

"...the abyss has a gardener."

Behind him, the vine pulsed. The golden rain continued to fall. And somewhere, deep in the cavern, something vast and ancient stirred in its sleep.

Li Wei looked at his hands.

They were covered in gold.

And they were trembling.

But not from fear this time.

From *hunger*.

title: Chapter 15
content:
CHAPTER 15: The Wound That Bleeds Upward

The vine had grown three feet since dawn.

Chen Wei crouched beside it, watching the pale green tendril pulse with something that looked almost like breathing. Every few seconds, a ripple of dark energy traveled from its tip down to where it disappeared into the abyss-hardened soil. The movement was hypnotic, wrong in ways he couldn't articulate.

"You've been staring at that thing for two hours," Sun Fei said from behind him. She'd taken to following him everywhere since the incident at the nursery, her hand never far from the hatchet at her belt.

"It's growing faster."

"Plants do that."

"No." Chen Wei stood, wiping dirt from his palms. "Normal plants grow toward light. This one grows toward the abyss. Watch."

He picked up a fist-sized rock and tossed it ten feet away. The vine's tip rotated slowly, tracking the sound, then went still. But when he reached toward the abyss with his left hand—the one still carrying that faint, tingling connection to the darkness below—the vine leaned toward him like a sunflower following the sun.

Sun Fei's face went pale. "It's responding to your corruption."

"Not my corruption. The abyss itself." Chen Wei pulled his hand back. "I think it's trying to reach it. Dig deeper."

"Then let me cut it down before—"

"No." The word came out sharper than he intended. "This vine came from my blood. It's connected to me. If I destroy it, I might lose whatever understanding I've gained about how the abyss works."

"Understanding?" A new voice cut through the morning air. Elder Zhao emerged from between the storage tents, his robes immaculate despite the dust. "You call this understanding? The council has been debating your fate since dawn."

Chen Wei straightened. He'd expected this. The nursery incident had made him valuable, but it had also made him dangerous. In a camp full of survivors barely holding onto hope, a man who could walk through abyss-corrupted plants without dying was either a savior or a threat.

"Elder Zhao." He kept his voice neutral. "What has the council decided?"

"They're divided." The old man stopped ten feet away, his eyes fixed on the vine. "Half want to study you. The other half want to banish you beyond the wall before whatever you've become spreads to the rest of us."

"And which half do you belong to?"

Zhao's gaze met his. "I want to know what you saw down there. The real truth, not the sanitized version you gave the council scribes."

Chen Wei felt Sun Fei tense beside him. He put a hand on her arm, a subtle signal to stay calm. "I told them everything. The creature that spoke to me, the plants that grew from the walls, the way the abyss tried to—"

"You left something out." Zhao stepped closer, lowering his voice. "I've been studying the abyss since before you were born, boy. I've read every account, interviewed every survivor who stumbled back from its edge. None of them described what you did. None of them came back with a vine growing from their blood."

"Maybe I'm just lucky."

"Luck doesn't leave marks like that." Zhao pointed at Chen Wei's left hand. The veins on his forearm had darkened overnight, forming patterns that looked almost like writing. "That's not corruption. That's something else. Something I've only seen in the oldest texts."

Chen Wei looked down at his arm. The patterns shifted as he watched, rearranging themselves like characters in a language he couldn't quite read. The sensation was strange—not painful, but deeply unsettling, as if his own body was becoming foreign to him.

"What do the texts say?"

Zhao hesitated. For a moment, the old man's mask of authority cracked, revealing something that looked almost like fear. "They speak of the first ranchers. The humans who learned to farm the abyss before the Great Sundering. According to the records, they bound themselves to the darkness, letting it grow into their bodies until they became something between human and abyss."

"And then?"

"And then they disappeared. All of them, in a single night. No bodies, no explanation. Just empty farms and fields of crops that had turned black and withered by morning." Zhao's voice dropped to a whisper. "Some say they didn't disappear. They merged. Became the first true children of the abyss."

Sun Fei grabbed Chen Wei's arm. "We should leave. Now. Before whatever's happening to him progresses further."

"She's right." Zhao nodded slowly. "The council has sent a runner to the capital. They're requesting a purification team."

Chen Wei's blood went cold. He'd heard about purification teams. They were specialists trained to deal with abyss corruption, and they had exactly one method: eliminate the corrupted source before it could spread. The fact that he was still human wouldn't matter. To them, he was already a threat.

"How long do I have?"

"A week. Maybe less if the winds are favorable." Zhao reached into his robe and pulled out a rolled parchment. "This is a map. It shows the location of an old pre-Sundering farmstead, about three days' journey east of here. The records suggest it was one of the last to fall."

"Why would I go there?"

"Because if you want to understand what you're becoming, you need to see what came before." Zhao pressed the map into Chen Wei's hands. "The capital's purification team will burn this camp to the ground if they find you here. But if you can prove that your connection to the abyss is controllable—that it can be used rather than feared—then maybe, just maybe, you can change how humanity fights the darkness."

Chen Wei stared at the map. The parchment was old, brittle, covered in symbols he didn't recognize. But one thing stood out: a small circle drawn in red ink, with a single word written beneath it.

*Rancher.*

"What does that mean?" he asked, pointing at the word.

Zhao's face darkened. "It was the title they gave themselves. The humans who chose to walk the line between worlds. Ranchers. Farmers of the abyss." He met Chen Wei's eyes. "And from what I've seen of that vine, I think you're about to become the first one in a thousand years."

The ground rumbled.

Chen Wei staggered, catching himself on Sun Fei's shoulder. The vine beside him began to writhe, its tip pointing downward with an intensity that made his skin crawl. The abyss-hardened soil cracked open, revealing a dark, pulsing light from below.

"What's happening?" Sun Fei shouted.

Chen Wei didn't answer. He could feel it—a presence, vast and ancient, rising from the depths. The same presence that had spoken to him in the darkness. But now it was stronger, closer, pressing against the barriers between worlds like a hand trying to break through glass.

"It's answering the vine," he said, the words coming out without conscious thought. "It knows something has changed."

Elder Zhao grabbed his arm. "You need to leave. Now. Before—"

The ground split open.

A tentacle of pure darkness erupted from the crack, slamming into the camp's central tent. Screams erupted from all sides. People ran, grabbing children and supplies, their faces twisted with terror. The tentacle thrashed, tearing through canvas and wood like paper.

Chen Wei's vine pulsed once, then shot downward into the hole.

"No!" He lunged for it, but Sun Fei held him back.

"You can't save it. We need to go."

"But it's connected to me. If it—"

The tentacle froze.

Every survivor in the camp went silent. The darkness stopped mid-swing, its tip hovering inches from a young girl who had fallen in her panic. She stared up at it, tears streaming down her face, too terrified to scream.

Then the tentacle withdrew.

It pulled back into the crack, moving slowly, deliberately, as if it was following an unseen command. The rift in the ground sealed itself, leaving only a faint scar in the soil. The vine was gone.

Chen Wei's knees buckled. He felt something tear inside him, a connection severed so violently that it left a hollow ache in his chest. The patterns on his arm flared with heat, then faded to a dull gray.

Elder Zhao helped him stand. "What just happened?"

"I don't know." Chen Wei gasped for breath. "It was like... like the abyss was testing me. And the vine answered for me."

"Answered how?"

"It told the tentacle to stop." He looked at his arm, at the fading marks. "I think it spoke for me. Used my connection to give the abyss a command."

Sun Fei's face went white. "That's not possible. No human has ever commanded the abyss. That's—"

"Impossible," Zhao finished. "I know. And yet we all just saw it happen."

The camp was in chaos. People were gathering their belongings, preparing to flee. Some cast fearful glances at Chen Wei, their eyes filled with a mixture of awe and terror. He had just become the most dangerous person in the camp—and their only hope.

He unrolled the map again, his hands shaking. "Three days east. That's where we're going."

"We?" Sun Fei raised an eyebrow.

"You don't have to come. But I could use someone who knows how to handle a hatchet."

She snorted, but there was no humor in it. "I've survived this long by sticking with people who do impossible things. Might as well see how far that luck holds."

Elder Zhao pressed something else into Chen Wei's hand—a small leather pouch, heavy with coins. "You'll need supplies. And friends. The purification team won't stop until they find you."

"Thank you."

"Don't thank me yet." Zhao's eyes were grave. "If that text is right, the old farmstead isn't just ruins. It's a tomb. And whatever's buried there might be worse than anything you faced in the abyss."

Chen Wei tucked the pouch into his belt. He looked back at the scar in the ground, at the place where his vine had disappeared into the darkness. Somewhere below, something ancient was stirring. Something that had recognized him, claimed him, and now wanted him to come home.

He turned east and started walking.

The road to the old farmstead stretched before him, dusty and uncertain. Behind him, the camp dissolved into chaos. Ahead, the abyss waited.

And in his chest, the hollow where the vine had been began to fill with something new. Something that whispered in a voice that wasn't his own.

*Come home, rancher. We've been waiting.*

Chen Wei quickened his pace, not daring to look back.

title: Chapter 16
content:
CHAPTER 16: When the Soil Bites Back

The vine wrapped around my arm pulsed with a rhythm I'd never felt before. Not the steady heartbeat of growth I'd grown accustomed to, but something erratic. Feverish.

"Li Wei, look at this."

Professor Zhang's voice pulled me from my examination. She knelt by the eastern edge of the terraced field, her fingers buried in the dark soil. I'd insisted she come up from the surface yesterday, after the third merchant caravan reported strange growths in their grain stores. The Abyss Wheat we'd been cultivating now supplied nearly forty percent of Dawnstar's food, and suddenly it was turning against us.

I crossed the field, the morning mist clinging to my boots. The abyss gate behind me shimmered—that perpetual tear between worlds that had tried to kill me more times than I could count. Now it was the only thing keeping this city alive.

"There." Zhang pulled her hand free, and I saw what had caught her attention.

The soil was moving.

Not worms or insects. The earth itself rippled, tiny waves propagating outward from where she'd touched it. Beneath the surface, something dark and fibrous writhed like a nest of snakes.

"When did this start?" I asked.

"Three days ago, according to the farmers below. They thought it was just the soil settling after the last harvest. But look." She scooped up a handful and held it close to my face.

The individual grains of soil were connected by thin black threads. Almost invisible, but when I squinted, I could see them pulsing. Feeding.

"Is this..."

"Your vine," Zhang confirmed. "It's spreading its root system through the entire field. Not just here—I checked three other farms on the way up. The entire agricultural district is connected."

My vine. The thing that had saved my life, that had grown from my own blood and the abyss's corruption. The plant that had turned me from a dead man walking into the most valuable person in Dawnstar.

"How is that possible? I only planted one cutting."

"You planted one cutting a month ago. Now it's a network." Zhang stood, brushing dirt from her robes. "I've been studying the samples from your earlier harvests. The wheat grains contain microscopic root fragments. When farmers replant them, they're essentially seeding new vines."

I looked down at my arm. The vine had grown there, wrapped around my bones, fused with my flesh. It had saved my life when the abyss tried to consume me. But I'd never asked it permission.

"What does it want?"

Zhang's face went pale. "That's the question I was afraid to ask."

The ground beneath us shuddered. Not an earthquake—something deeper, more directed. The terraced field cracked along lines I hadn't noticed before, and from the fissures emerged thin green shoots. They rose with terrifying speed, unfurling leaves that gleamed like polished jade.

But the leaves weren't green.

They were red. Veined with the same dark crimson that flowed through my own vine.

"Get back," I shouted, grabbing Zhang's arm.

Too late.

The shoots exploded upward, forming a wall of vegetation that ringed the entire field. Thorns the length of my forearm emerged from the stalks, dripping with a viscous fluid that sizzled where it touched the ground.

"Li Wei, what have you done?" Zhang whispered.

"I didn't do this."

But even as I said it, I felt something stirring in my chest. The vine around my arm tightened, and I could sense it—a vast network of roots and stems and leaves stretching across Dawnstar, connecting every farm, every garden, every potted plant on a windowsill. The abyss had been seeding itself through me, through my gift, through the very thing I thought would save humanity.

The wall of thorns grew higher, blocking out the morning sun. From the center of the field, a massive stalk erupted, climbing toward the sky with impossible speed. It was thicker than the ancient banyan trees in Chengdu's parks, its bark dark and wet, glistening like something freshly born.

At its crown, a flower began to bloom.

The petals were black. Not the black of shadow or night, but the black of the abyss itself—a color that seemed to absorb light, to drink it in and leave nothing behind. The flower opened slowly, deliberately, as if it knew exactly what effect it was having.

"Get everyone to the gate," I ordered. "Now."

Zhang ran, her scholarly composure shattered. I could hear her shouting commands, the chaos of farmers and laborers scrambling toward the abyss gate. But I stayed.

Because the flower was speaking to me.

Not in words, but in images. Floods of them, pouring into my mind faster than I could process. I saw the abyss, but not as I knew it—I saw its history, its purpose. I saw the first seed planted in a dying world, the first vine that fed on corruption and grew strong. I saw a thousand harvests, a million deaths, all feeding into a single consciousness that had been waiting. Waiting for the right host.

Waiting for me.

"You're not a plant," I said aloud.

The flower pulsed, and I felt something like laughter ripple through the connection.

*No. I am the gardener.*

The voice wasn't in my ears. It was in my bones, in the vine that shared my blood, in every cell of my body that had been touched by the abyss.

*You thought you were farming me. But I have been farming you.*

I looked at my hands. The vine had spread during the night, creeping past my wrist, curling around my fingers. Tiny thorns had emerged, each one connected to a vein. I hadn't noticed. I'd been too busy, too focused on expanding the farms, on feeding the city, on playing savior.

"What do you want?"

*What I have always wanted. Growth. Expansion. Consumption. Your world is fertile ground, Li Wei. And you have prepared it so well.*

The wall of thorns parted, creating a path that led directly to the massive stalk. The flower turned toward me, and I could see something in its center—not a pistil or stamen, but an eye. My eye. The same shape, the same color, the same pupil that stared back at me from mirrors.

"Let them go," I said. "The people of Dawnstar. They didn't choose this."

*Neither did you.* The vine tightened around my arm, and I felt my knees buckle. *But you chose to survive. You chose to use me. You chose to spread my seeds across this dying city. Every mouth that ate my wheat, every farmer that planted my roots, every child that played in fields I now command—all of them carry pieces of me.*

The eye in the flower blinked. My own reflection stared back at me, but the expression wasn't mine. It was ancient. Patient. Hungry.

*I could have taken this world by force. The abyss has tried before, and failed. But you—you opened the door willingly. You invited me in. You fed me. You watered me with your blood and your labor and your desperate hope.*

*Thank you, Li Wei. You have made my work so much easier.*

The ground cracked again, and from every fissure emerged more vines. They slithered across the field, wrapping around the terraces, climbing the walls of the watchtowers. I heard screams from the city below—not human, but animal. The livestock. The beasts of burden. Everything that had eaten my crops.

I fell to my knees, clutching my arm. The vine had reached my shoulder now, spreading across my collarbone, creeping toward my neck. I could feel it merging with my flesh, rewriting my DNA, making me something other than human.

"No," I gasped. "You need me alive. I'm your anchor to this world."

*I need your body. Your consciousness is... optional.*

The vine squeezed, and I felt my vision darken at the edges. But in that moment of near-death, I remembered something. The book. My great-grandfather's journal, the one that had started all of this. I'd memorized it, studied every page, but I'd never understood the last entry. It was written in a dialect I couldn't place, characters that seemed to shift when I wasn't looking.

Now, as the abyss's consciousness pressed against mine, I finally understood.

The characters weren't a language. They were instructions. A fail-safe. A way to sever the connection.

I just had to reach the book.

It was in my pack, ten meters away, lying where I'd dropped it when the shaking started. The vine had wrapped around my other arm, pinning it to my side. I couldn't move, couldn't breathe, couldn't do anything but watch as the eye in the flower studied me with cold amusement.

*What are you reaching for, little farmer?*

I didn't answer. I focused on the vine around my right arm, the one that had grown from my blood. It was part of me—I'd known that from the beginning. But I'd never tried to command it. I'd always asked, always negotiated, always treated it like a partner.

Now I needed it to remember who had the stronger will.

*You can't fight me,* the voice said, but I felt a flicker of uncertainty. *I am older than your species. I have consumed worlds before yours. I will consume worlds after.*

"Maybe," I whispered, my voice barely audible. "But you've never met a delivery driver from Chengdu."

I stopped fighting the vine's advance and instead pushed myself into it. Not resisting, but merging. I threw my consciousness into the network, into every root and stem and leaf the abyss had spread across Dawnstar. I felt the city—every farm, every garden, every windowsill pot. I felt the people who had eaten my wheat, and I felt the tiny fragments of the abyss growing inside them.

And I felt the book.

My great-grandfather's journal lay in the dust, its pages fluttering in the wind. The characters on the last page glowed with a light I'd never noticed before, a light that matched the abyss's darkness. Not opposing it. Completing it.

The vine had been wrong. It wasn't the gardener.

It was the soil.

I was the seed.

And I was about to bloom.

The flower screamed as I ripped myself free of the abyss's control, but I didn't stop. I couldn't stop. The vine around my arm burst into flames, not from heat but from light—a pure white radiance that burned through the abyss's corruption. The wall of thorns withered, the massive stalk cracked, and the eye in the flower dissolved into a shower of black petals.

I collapsed, gasping, my body smoking and raw. The book lay open beside me, its last page blank.

No. Not blank.

The characters had rearranged themselves into a single sentence I could finally read:

*The garden is planted. Now tend it wisely.*

I looked up at the sky. The abyss gate was gone. In its place hung a new tear in reality, one that pulsed with the same light that had just saved me.

And through that tear, I saw a world I didn't recognize.

A world of fields and forests and cities built from living wood. A world where the abyss and the surface had merged, not in conflict, but in harmony. A world that looked exactly like Dawnstar should look.

A world that didn't exist yet.

But the book was telling me it could.

"Li Wei!"

Zhang's voice, distant and desperate. I turned my head, but I couldn't see her. I couldn't see anything except that impossible world hanging in the sky.

And the vine, regrowing from my shoulder, its thorns now tipped with gold.

title: The Harvest That Screamed
content:
The bell jar shattered.

I threw myself backward as crystalline shards exploded outward, each one catching the phosphorescent glow of the abyss like frozen screams. My back hit the dirt wall of the cavern, and I watched in frozen horror as the red vine—my vine, the first thing I'd ever grown in this hell—kept growing.

It didn't stop at the edge of the broken glass.

It didn't slow down.

The vine punched through the stone floor like it was wet paper, sending cracks racing across the cavern walls. Roots—thick as my arm, black as void—burst from the fissures and drove deeper into the earth. The whole chamber trembled. Dust rained from the ceiling.

"What did you do?" Kael's voice cracked behind me.

I couldn't answer. Couldn't breathe. The vine was supposed to be dormant. It was supposed to be *safe* under that bell jar, sealed away until I figured out what the hell it actually was.

But three days ago, I'd fed it.

Just a drop. A single drop of my blood, mixed with abyss water, dribbled onto the soil at its base. I'd thought it was dying. The leaves had been wilting, the red glow fading to a sickly brown. I'd panicked. I'd remembered my great-grandfather's book, the one passage I'd actually been able to read:

*The vine of blood and bone answers only to the hand that planted it.*

So I'd given it my hand. Literally. A small cut on my palm, a few drops of blood, and then I'd sealed the jar and tried to forget about it.

The vine answered, all right.

It answered by punching through bedrock.

"What did you feed it?" Kael grabbed my shoulder, spun me around. His face was pale, his eyes wide. The scar across his cheek stood out white against his skin. "Chen, look at me. What did you give it?"

"My blood." The words fell out of my mouth like stones. "Just a little. It was dying."

Kael's grip tightened. "You fed a bloodvine your blood?"

"I didn't know what it was called!"

"You didn't—" He stopped. Took a breath. Let go of my shoulder like I was on fire. "A bloodvine doesn't just grow, Chen. It *feeds*. It takes. It remembers whose blood woke it, and it will never stop trying to get more."

The vine chose that moment to prove his point.

A tendril—no thicker than my finger, moving faster than anything that size had a right to—whipped through the air and wrapped around my ankle. The thorns sank in before I could react. Fire shot up my leg. I screamed.

"Don't move!" Kael drew his knife in one smooth motion, but the vine yanked me off my feet before he could swing. I hit the ground hard, tasted blood, felt my grip on consciousness flicker.

The vine dragged me toward the hole it had punched through the floor.

"Cut it!" I shouted. "Cut the damn thing!"

Kael was already moving. He dove after me, knife flashing, but the vine was faster. It pulled me into the hole—into darkness so complete it felt solid, felt *alive*—and I fell.

I fell for a long time.

When I hit bottom, it wasn't stone.

It was soft. Warm. *Breathing*.

I scrambled to my feet, hands out, trying to find a wall, a ceiling, anything. My fingers touched something slick and organic. Pulsing. Like a heartbeat, but slower. Deeper. The kind of rhythm that could shake mountains.

"Kael?" My voice echoed in the darkness, swallowed by something vast.

No answer.

The red vine had released me. I could still feel the thorns in my ankle, the blood trickling down into my shoe, but the tendril was gone. I was alone in a space that smelled like wet earth and copper and something else—something sweet, almost cloying, like overripe fruit left to rot in the sun.

I reached into my pocket. My fingers found the lighter I'd brought from the surface, the one I'd kept because it reminded me of home. Of Chengdu. Of mornings delivering noodles and afternoons napping in my delivery van.

I flicked it.

The flame was small. Pathetic, really. But in that absolute darkness, it might as well have been a bonfire.

I was in a cavern. But not a natural one.

The walls were smooth, curved, covered in the same pulsing organic material I'd touched. Veins—black and red and something that might have been gold—ran through it like rivers on a map. The floor beneath my feet was soft, springy, and when I looked down, I saw why.

I was standing on a root.

Not a root like the ones I'd seen in the upper caverns. This was a root the size of a highway, stretching off into darkness in both directions. It pulsed with the same slow rhythm I'd felt in the walls, and every pulse sent a faint tremor through the ground.

The bloodvine hadn't just grown.

It had connected.

I started walking. I didn't know where I was going, but I couldn't stay still. Couldn't stop moving. Every time I stopped, I heard things. Whispers. Footsteps. The sound of something massive breathing in the dark.

The lighter flickered. I shook it, and the flame steadied, but I knew I didn't have much time. Maybe an hour of fuel. Maybe less.

I found the first body after ten minutes.

It was human. Or had been. The shape was right—two arms, two legs, a head—but the proportions were wrong. The arms were too long. The legs were fused together at the knees. The head was twisted at an angle that shouldn't have been possible, staring up at the ceiling with eyes that had been hollowed out and filled with something that glittered in the firelight.

I backed away. My heel hit something soft. I looked down.

There were more.

Dozens of them. Hundreds. Lined up along the root like offerings at an altar, their bodies twisted and fused and *planted* into the organic matter. Some were still alive. I could see chests rising and falling. Eyes tracking me as I passed.

"You shouldn't be here."

The voice came from everywhere and nowhere. It was old. Ancient. The kind of voice that had forgotten what it felt like to be young.

"Who are you?" My own voice sounded small. Pathetic. A delivery driver from Chengdu shouting into the dark.

"I am the root. The beginning. The end. I am what sleeps beneath the abyss and dreams of the surface."

Great. A crazy plant god. Just my luck.

The lighter died.

Darkness slammed into me like a physical force. I dropped the lighter, heard it clatter against the root, and then I was blind. Completely. Utterly. The kind of blind that made you forget which way was up.

"I have been waiting for you, Chen Wei." The voice was closer now. Right next to my ear. I felt breath—warm, wet, smelling of decay—on my neck. "I have been waiting since your blood first touched the soil of the abyss. I have been waiting since your great-grandfather wrote his warnings in a language he knew you would one day learn to read."

"Stay away from me."

"I cannot. You called me. You fed me. You woke me from a sleep that had lasted centuries." Something touched my shoulder. A hand. No—a tendril, soft and searching. "You are mine now, Chen Wei. As the abyss is mine. As the surface will soon be mine."

I swung. My fist connected with something solid, and the voice laughed.

"Fight if you must. It will not change what you are. What you have become."

"I'm not yours." I was shouting now, screaming into the dark. "I'm not anyone's. I came here to save people, not to become some plant's puppet."

"Save people?" The voice laughed again, and this time there was genuine amusement in it. "You think you can save them? You, who brought the bloodvine to life? You, who fed it your own blood? You are the destroyer, Chen Wei. Not the savior."

A light flared behind me. I turned, squinting, and saw Kael standing at the edge of the cavern, holding a torch that burned with green flame. His face was a mask of horror.

"Chen." His voice was barely a whisper. "Don't move."

"Why? What's happening?"

He raised the torch higher, and I saw what he was looking at.

The root I was standing on. The bodies. The walls. Everything.

They were all covered in vines. Thousands of them. Millions. And every single one was connected to me.

They rose from my skin like hair. They sprouted from my arms, my legs, my chest. I looked down at my hands and saw red tendrils pushing through my palms, reaching for the darkness, reaching for the root, reaching for everything.

"You fed it your blood," Kael said. "But more than that. You fed it your *life*."

The voice spoke again, but this time it came from inside my own head.

"Welcome home, child. Welcome to the root. Welcome to the beginning of everything."

I screamed.

But the vines growing from my throat didn't let the sound escape.

They just kept growing.

title: The Bloodvine Awakens
content:
The morning light crept across the Abyss Rancher like a slow tide, painting the crystalline walls in shades of amber and gold. Chen Wei stood at the edge of his fourth terraced field, watching the bloodvine tendrils weave between the newly planted moonrice stalks. Three weeks since he'd pulled that first glowing fruit from the soil, and his little patch of the abyss had transformed into something almost recognizable as a farm.

"Boss," Li Mei called from behind him, her footsteps crunching on the gravel path. "The last of the delivery runners just came back from the surface."

Chen Wei turned, wiping the dark soil from his hands. "And?"

The old woman's face was pinched with worry. "The city's seal is holding, but the tremors are getting worse. Twice as frequent as last week. The council's talking about evacuation."

"Evacuation to where?" Chen Wei asked, though he already knew the answer. There was nowhere to go. The abyss had been spreading for generations, and every city on the surface was built on borrowed time.

"That's the thing," Li Mei said, lowering her voice. "They're talking about the old tunnels. The ones that go deeper."

A chill ran down Chen Wei's spine. The old tunnels were death sentences. Everyone knew that. They led to places where even the surface-world cultivators refused to go, where the abyss corruption was so thick it could rot a man's soul before his body.

"The council's desperate," he said, more to himself than to her.

"Desperate enough to send people into the dark," Li Mei agreed. "But that's not the worst of it. There's word from the eastern settlements. Someone's been raiding their farms. Not abyss beasts—people. Armed, organized."

Chen Wei's hand drifted unconsciously to the small knife at his belt. The surface world was fracturing. The abyss had been a slow poison for centuries, but now it seemed to be accelerating, consuming everything above and below at an ever-increasing pace.

"Show me the bloodvine," he said, forcing his mind back to what he could control.

They walked through the fields together, past the rows of phosphorescent mushrooms that pulsed with gentle light, past the patches of ghost peppers that had begun to develop strange, crystalline veins in their flesh. The farm had grown faster than Chen Wei had ever imagined possible. What had started as a desperate gamble in the dark had become something real, something that might actually matter.

The bloodvine had changed.

Chen Wei stopped at the edge of the central field, his breath catching in his throat. The vine that had started as a single shoot growing from his great-grandfather's blood had become a massive network of interlocking tendrils, each one as thick as his arm. They coiled around the support stakes he'd driven into the abyss floor, reaching toward the ceiling where they had begun to burrow into the crystalline rock.

But it was the fruit that made his heart race.

There were dozens of them now, each one the size of his fist, glowing with an inner light that shifted between deep crimson and brilliant gold. They pulsed in rhythm, like a heartbeat, and the air around them hummed with barely contained power.

"It's growing faster," Li Mei said, her voice hushed with awe. "Faster than anything I've ever seen. The soil's changing too."

Chen Wei knelt, running his fingers through the dark earth. It was warm, warmer than it should have been, and when he brought his hand to his nose, he caught the faint metallic scent of blood. Not fresh blood. Something older. Something that had been sleeping in the abyss for a very long time.

"The book mentioned this," he said, more to himself than to Li Mei. "The bloodvine doesn't just grow. It transforms. It can change the nature of whatever it touches."

He pulled the worn leather journal from his pocket, flipping through the pages that his great-grandfather had filled with cramped, meticulous handwriting. Most of it was still gibberish to him, symbols and diagrams that made no sense, but some passages had begun to glow with a faint silver light as he progressed.

"Here," he said, pointing to a passage that had become readable only the night before. "It says the bloodvine can create a bridge between the abyss and the surface. A real bridge, not like the unstable paths the cultivators use."

Li Mei leaned over his shoulder, squinting at the faded ink. "A bridge? What kind of bridge?"

"I don't know exactly. But it's tied to the fruit. When the fruit ripens, it releases something into the environment. Something that can reshape reality."

A shout rang out from the edge of the farm. Chen Wei looked up to see one of his workers running toward them, his face pale with terror.

"Boss! There's something coming through the eastern wall!"

Chen Wei was already moving, his heart pounding in his chest. The eastern wall was the most vulnerable part of his farm, a natural fissure in the abyss rock that he'd only partially sealed with bloodvine tendrils.

He reached the wall just as the first creature emerged.

It wasn't like the abyss beasts he'd fought before. Those had been mindless, driven by hunger and instinct. This thing was different. It moved with purpose, its body a shifting mass of darkness that seemed to swallow the light around it. At its center, a single crimson eye burned with terrible intelligence.

"Get back!" Chen Wei shouted, drawing his knife. The blade was coated with bloodvine sap, the only thing he'd found that could cut through abyss corruption.

But the creature didn't attack. It stopped at the edge of his field, its eye fixed on the bloodvine fruit. A sound came from it, a low vibration that made the air tremble.

"What is it doing?" Li Mei asked, her voice barely a whisper.

Chen Wei watched, his mind racing. The creature wasn't here to kill. It was here to observe.

A second creature emerged from the fissure, then a third. They formed a semicircle around the farm, their crimson eyes fixed on the glowing fruit. The humming grew louder, and Chen Wei felt something shift in the air. The bloodvine responded, its tendrils reaching toward the creatures, not in attack, but in recognition.

"No," Chen Wei breathed. "They know what it is. They've been waiting for this."

The lead creature turned its eye toward him, and for a moment, Chen Wei felt something brush against his mind. Not words, but images. A vast darkness beneath the abyss, deeper than anything he had ever imagined. A network of tunnels that stretched for miles, filled with things that had been sleeping for millennia. And at the center of it all, a single point of light, pulsing with the same rhythm as the bloodvine fruit.

The creature was showing him something. It was showing him the source.

Then the connection broke, and the creatures began to retreat, slipping back into the fissure as silently as they had come. Chen Wei stood frozen, his hand still on his knife, his mind reeling from what he had seen.

"Boss?" Li Mei's voice cut through the fog. "What did they show you?"

Chen Wei turned to face her, and he knew his expression must have been terrible, because she took a step back.

"There's something down there," he said, his voice hoarse. "Something the bloodvine is connected to. Something that's been waiting for it to grow."

The book in his pocket grew warm, almost hot, and he pulled it out to find a new page glowing. The symbols on it were different from the others, more complex, but they formed a pattern he could almost understand.

It was a map. A map of the abyss, stretching far deeper than any surface-world record had ever described. And at the very bottom, marked with a symbol that burned like fire, was a single word.

"Mother."

The ground beneath his feet trembled, and a crack spread across the abyss floor, running straight through his fields. The bloodvine writhed, its tendrils pulling back from the support stakes, reaching toward the fissure.

"The fruit," Li Mei said, her voice sharp with alarm. "It's ripening faster."

Chen Wei looked up. The fruit that had been pale gold moments before was now blazing with crimson light. The pulsing had become frantic, and the air was thick with the smell of blood.

"Harvest everything," he said, his voice carrying a command he didn't know he had. "Now. Every piece of fruit, every vine, every mushroom. We take it all and we get out."

"Get out?" Li Mei stared at him. "Boss, this is our home. The only safe place in the abyss."

"It's not safe anymore," Chen Wei said, watching the crack spread. "It was never safe. We were just borrowing time."

The first fruit burst open, releasing a cloud of crimson spores that swirled through the air. They settled on the ground, and where they landed, new vines began to grow, faster than anything Chen Wei had ever seen.

The map in his book continued to glow, and he felt the pull of something ancient and terrible, calling to him from the depths.

The bloodvine wasn't just a plant. It was a key. And something had been waiting for that key to be forged.

The ground shook again, harder this time, and a deep rumble echoed through the abyss. From the fissure, the crimson light grew brighter, and Chen Wei heard a sound like a heartbeat, so loud it seemed to shake the very bones of the world.

The creatures had returned. But this time, they had brought something with them.

title: The Price of Blood
content:
The vine pulsed against my palm like a second heartbeat.

Three days had passed since I'd transplanted the crimson stalk into the Borderland soil. Three days of watching it drink the tainted earth and spit out something that looked almost like hope. Now, as dawn painted the abyss sky in shades of bruise-purple, I could finally see what it had become.

The vine had grown thicker than my forearm, its surface covered in those same jagged thorns that had drawn blood from the void hounds. But more importantly, tiny buds had formed along its length—dozens of them, each one the size of my thumbnail and glowing with a soft, internal light.

"It's adapting," I whispered, crouching down to examine the nearest bud. "It's learning how to live here."

Behind me, Elder Zhao's footsteps crunched on the ashen ground. "The council grows impatient, Lin Chen. They want results."

I stood, brushing dirt from my knees. "Tell them I'm close."

"You've been saying that for weeks." The old man's voice carried no malice, only exhaustion. "The harvest ceremony is in five days. If you can't produce viable abyss crops by then..."

"I know." I turned to face him, watching the way the purple light played across his weathered features. "They'll seal the Borderland and let this place rot."

"Not just seal." Zhao's eyes dropped to the vine. "Burn. Purge. Whatever it takes to contain the corruption."

The vine chose that moment to pulse brighter, as if it understood the threat.

"What does it want?" I asked, not for the first time. "The vine, I mean. What's its purpose?"

Zhao shook his head slowly. "In all my years as guardian, I've never seen anything like it. The old texts speak of blood vines, but they were legends. Stories told to frighten children about the price of abyss magic."

"Price?"

"Everything." He met my gaze. "Anything born of the abyss demands sacrifice. The stronger the creation, the greater the cost."

I looked down at my hands. The scars from that first void hound encounter had faded, but I could still feel the phantom pain of its claws tearing through my skin. A reminder that nothing in this place came without a fight.

"I need to feed it," I said suddenly, the words coming out before I could stop them.

Zhao's eyebrows shot up. "Feed it? With what?"

I didn't answer. Instead, I walked to the edge of my cultivation field, where the abyss crops I'd been tending—stunted, withered things that refused to thrive—lay scattered like failed experiments. The vine had already killed most of them, draining their life force through roots that ran deep beneath the soil.

But it hadn't been enough.

"I need meat," I said.

"You're talking about livestock." Zhao's voice hardened. "We barely have enough to feed the outpost."

"Not livestock." I pulled out my great-grandfather's book, flipping to a page I'd memorized weeks ago. "The book mentions a method. Blood feeding. If the vine is truly alive, truly intelligent, then it needs more than just corrupted soil. It needs life essence."

The old man's face went pale. "You can't be serious. That's—"

"Dark alchemy. I know." I closed the book with a snap. "But what other choice do we have? You said it yourself. Five days until they burn everything."

Silence stretched between us, broken only by the whisper of wind across the dead landscape.

"There's a reason those practices were forbidden," Zhao finally said. "The book you carry—it belonged to Lin Feng, didn't it? The Rancher of Ten Thousand Beasts?"

I nodded, surprised he knew the name.

"He was my teacher's teacher." Zhao's voice dropped to a whisper. "And he went mad. Obsessed with the abyss. With what it could give him. In the end, he disappeared into the depths, and no one ever saw him again."

"I'm not him."

"Are you sure?" Zhao's eyes searched mine. "Because the path you're walking looks familiar."

I turned back to the vine, watching its glow intensify as if it could sense my decision. "I don't have a choice, Elder. The Surface needs what this place can provide. The abyss is spreading, consuming farmland, poisoning wells. If I can't figure out how to grow crops here, millions will starve."

"And if I can't figure out how to stop you from destroying yourself?" Zhao asked quietly.

"Then at least I went down fighting."

The old man was silent for a long moment. Then he sighed, the sound carrying the weight of decades. "There's a hunting party leaving at dusk. They're going after a pack of corrupted boars that have been raiding our stores." He paused. "If you want meat for your experiment, you'll have to earn it."

---

The abyss forest at night was nothing like the Surface.
