Here, the trees grew twisted and black, their branches reaching toward the purple sky like grasping fingers. Bioluminescent fungi covered every surface, casting everything in an eerie green glow that made the world look like an underwater nightmare.

I followed the hunting party through the underbrush, my heart hammering against my ribs. There were six of us total—four soldiers from the outpost, Elder Zhao, and myself. Each of the soldiers carried crossbows loaded with abyss-iron bolts, weapons designed to pierce corrupted hide.

"We split up," Captain Wei said, his voice barely a whisper. "Three groups of two. Boars will be near the water source. Standard pincer maneuver."

I found myself paired with Zhao, which I suspected was intentional.

"Stay close," the old man said, moving through the darkness with surprising grace. "And whatever you do, don't get separated."

We walked for what felt like hours, the forest growing denser and darker the deeper we went. The fungi gave way to something else—a black moss that seemed to absorb light rather than emit it. I could barely see my hand in front of my face.

"How do you navigate?" I asked, stumbling over a root.

"Feel." Zhao's voice came from somewhere ahead. "The abyss has a rhythm. A pulse. Learn to read it, and you'll never get lost."

I tried to focus, to sense whatever it was he sensed, but all I felt was the oppressive weight of the place pressing down on me. The vine in my pocket—I'd brought a cutting with me—vibrated against my thigh, excited by the proximity to its native environment.

"There." Zhao stopped, pointing into the darkness. "Do you see it?"

I squinted, and gradually, shapes emerged from the gloom. Three of them, massive and hunched, their tusks gleaming with something that might have been metal. The corrupted boars.

They were twice the size of normal boars, their hides covered in chitinous plates that looked like armor. When they moved, I could hear the grinding of bone against bone, a sound that set my teeth on edge.

"How do we—"

The first crossbow bolt took the largest boar in the eye.

It didn't die.

Instead, it let out a roar that shook the trees and charged, its companions following close behind. The other hunters had engaged, their bolts finding chinks in the beasts' armor, but the boars kept coming, driven by something beyond pain.

I reached for my knife—the same one I'd used to skin that first void hound—but my hand came away empty. In my rush to join the hunt, I'd left it behind.

"Damn it."

The vine in my pocket pulsed, and suddenly I understood.

I pulled out the cutting, holding it before me like a weapon. It had grown in the hours since dawn, the buds now open, revealing petals of deepest crimson that seemed to drink the light around them.

"I don't know what I'm doing," I whispered.

But the vine did.

It lashed out, faster than I could follow, wrapping around the nearest boar's leg. The creature squealed as the thorns dug into its flesh, drawing blood that steamed in the cold air. And then the vine began to drink.

I felt it through my connection to the plant—a surge of raw life essence flooding through the cutting, into my palm, up my arm. It was intoxicating and terrifying, power and corruption intertwined.

"Lin Chen!" Zhao's voice cut through the haze. "Stop it! You don't know what you're—"

The boar collapsed, its body shriveling as the vine drained it dry. The other two beasts turned, sensing the shift in power, and for a moment, I saw something like fear in their corrupted eyes.

Then they charged.

I raised the vine, ready to meet them, when something else emerged from the darkness.

It was bigger than the boars, bigger than anything I'd seen in the abyss. A shape that moved like smoke and solid stone, with eyes that burned like dying stars. The boars tried to flee, but they were too slow. The thing consumed them in a single gulp, swallowing their corrupted forms whole.

And then it turned to face me.

"Lin Feng's blood," it said, its voice the sound of grinding mountains. "I thought I'd never smell you again."

The vine in my hand went cold.

And the world went dark.

title: The Bloodvine Blooms
content:
The scream came from the nursery.

Li Wei dropped the watering can and ran. His boots splashed through puddles of abyss-tainted water as he rounded the corner of the stone building. The sound wasn't human—it was something between a shriek and a grinding of metal, like two boulders crushing a living thing between them.

He burst through the door and stopped dead.

The bloodvine had grown.

In the three days since he'd planted the seed from the Abyss Rancher's book, it had remained a stubby, dark-red sprout no bigger than his thumb. Now it reached to his chest, its thick stalk pulsing with an inner light that cast red shadows across the room. Dozens of tendrils, each tipped with a razor-sharp thorn the length of his finger, writhed in the air like serpents searching for prey.

And wrapped in those tendrils was a shade.

The creature struggled, its formless black body shifting and twisting as the thorns pierced it. Where they touched, the shade's darkness turned to steam, dissolving into the air with that terrible screeching sound. The bloodvine's leaves—if you could call them leaves, being more like flaps of skin—vibrated with what Li Wei could only describe as hunger.

"What the hell..."

The shade dissolved completely. The bloodvine's tendrils retracted, and for a moment, the plant seemed to swell, its color deepening from dark red to something closer to fresh blood. A single flower bud formed at the top of the stalk, tight and closed, the size of his fist.

Li Wei's hands trembled as he pulled out the book. The pages had started to fill with new text over the last week, appearing in the same elegant script as the original entries. He flipped to the most recent page.

*The Bloodvine Sprout has matured. It has tasted its first prey. When the flower blooms, the true connection will be established.*

*Warning: The Bloodvine requires regular feeding. Starvation will cause it to seek sustenance from its master.*

"Heartwarming," Li Wei muttered. "Really makes you feel the love."

He looked around the nursery. The other plants—the glowcaps, the tremor grass, the small patch of abyss wheat—all seemed healthy. But the bloodvine had changed everything. He could feel it now, a presence at the edge of his awareness, like a second heartbeat pulsing beneath the earth.

Elder Chen was going to lose his mind.

---

The old man arrived at sunset, as he did every evening. He walked with a gnarled staff that was more root than wood, his white beard brushing against a chest covered in scars from a lifetime of fighting abyss creatures. He stopped in the doorway of the nursery and stared.

"That's not possible."

Li Wei sat on a stool, watching the bloodvine sway gently in the still air. The flower bud had grown larger, now the size of his head, and it pulsed with a steady rhythm. "That's what I said."

"The book said it would take months, maybe years." Elder Chen stepped closer, his eyes never leaving the plant. "You've had it for three days."

"I fed it a shade."

"You what?"

"It came through the wall." Li Wei pointed to a crack in the stone that hadn't been there before. "The barrier's weakening faster than we thought. It was either feed it to the plant or let it eat the glowcaps."

Elder Chen ran his hand over the crack. His fingers came away coated in a fine black dust that sizzled when it touched his skin. "The abyss is pressing harder. We've held this farm for forty years, but something's changing. The creatures are getting bolder, the corruption deeper."

"Then we need to change too." Li Wei stood, walking to the bloodvine. He didn't touch it—he'd learned that lesson yesterday when a tendril had wrapped around his wrist and drawn blood before he'd managed to pull free. "The book says this plant can connect the surface to the abyss. A real connection, not just the temporary portals the mages use."

"The mages would burn you alive for even suggesting that."

"The mages are losing." Li Wei's voice was flat. "You told me yourself. The kingdoms on the surface are falling one by one. The abyss is spreading faster than anyone can stop it. What's the point of following their rules if those rules lead to extinction?"

Elder Chen was silent for a long moment. Outside, the sun had fully set, and the abyss night had begun. The air grew cold, and the walls started to hum with the distant pressure of the creatures that swam through the darkness between worlds.

"There's a reason the old ranchers feared the bloodvine," Elder Chen said finally. "It doesn't just feed on abyss creatures. It feeds on everything. Given enough time and enough food, it would consume this entire farm, then the mountain, then the world."

"Then we don't give it that much time."

"You think you can control it?"

"I think I don't have a choice." Li Wei turned to face the old man. "You brought me here because the book chose me. You said I was the first new rancher in fifty years. Well, here I am. The book's showing me things, teaching me things that aren't in any of your records. The bloodvine isn't just a weapon—it's a door. A door that goes both ways."

Elder Chen's eyes narrowed. "What are you planning?"

"What the book tells me to."

The old man's staff slammed against the ground, and the shockwave sent ripples through the dirt. "You're a fool. The book is a tool, not a master. Your great-grandfather went mad trying to follow its instructions to the letter. He ended up trapped in the abyss, his own creations turning against him."

"And yet he still sent the book to find me." Li Wei touched the leather-bound volume at his belt. "He knew this day would come. He knew someone would have to finish what he started."

The bloodvine's flower bud pulsed brighter, and both men turned to look at it. A crack had formed along its surface, and a thin orange light leaked through, casting strange shadows across the room.

"It's blooming," Li Wei whispered.

The petals unfolded slowly, each one a dark red membrane that glistened with something like oil. The light intensified until they had to shield their eyes, and the temperature in the room shot up by twenty degrees. When the flower was fully open, Li Wei saw what lay at its center.

Not a pistil or stamen.

An eye.

The eye was human in shape but alien in color—a deep purple iris ringed with gold, the pupil a vertical slit that dilated as it focused on him. The bloodvine's tendrils reached out, not aggressively this time, but gently, as if offering an embrace.

And Li Wei heard a voice in his mind.

*Master.*

He stumbled back, nearly falling over his stool. Elder Chen caught his arm, steadying him.

"What is it?" the old man demanded. "What do you see?"

"Can't you hear it?"

"Hear what?"

The eye blinked. Once, twice, then focused on Li Wei with an intensity that made his skin crawl. The voice returned, clearer this time.

*I am your connection. I am your weapon. I am your door.*

*Feed me.*

"I need to feed it," Li Wei said, his voice distant, as if hearing himself from far away. "It needs more."

Elder Chen grabbed him by the shoulders, shaking him. "Snap out of it, boy. That plant is influencing you. The book warned about this—the bloodvine's will is strong, and it will try to dominate its master."

But even as the old man spoke, Li Wei could feel the truth of the plant's words. The bloodvine wasn't his enemy. It was his partner. The book had been telling him all along—the abyss couldn't be fought with swords and magic. It had to be farmed, cultivated, used.

The surface kingdoms were dying because they saw the abyss as an enemy to be destroyed. But you didn't destroy a field to harvest its crops. You nurtured it, fed it, and took what you needed.

"Let go of me." Li Wei pulled free of Elder Chen's grip. "I know what I'm doing."

"You're walking into a trap. The same trap your great-grandfather fell into."

"Then I'll walk out again." Li Wei turned to the bloodvine, meeting its gaze. "How much do you need?"

The eye blinked slowly, and the petals rustled. Images flooded his mind—a small shade lurking near the eastern wall, a cluster of abyss crawlers nesting in the collapsed tunnel, a larger creature, something with too many legs and too many teeth, hunting in the darkness beyond the farm's light.

*All of them,* the voice said. *Bring them to me. Let me grow strong enough to pierce the veil between worlds.*

"And then what?"

*Then you will see what your great-grandfather saw. The truth of the abyss. The way to save everything.*

Elder Chen's staff slammed again, harder this time. "I won't let you do this. You're the last hope of the ranchers, and you're about to throw it away for a plant's promises."

Li Wei looked at the old man, saw the fear and anger in his weathered face. Elder Chen had spent his whole life fighting the abyss, losing friends and family to its creatures, watching the world slowly crumble. He couldn't see what Li Wei saw—not because he was blind, but because he was too scarred by loss to trust any path that didn't end in destruction.

"I'm not throwing anything away." Li Wei walked to the wall, to the crack where the shade had entered. The air on the other side was cold, dark, and filled with the sounds of creatures moving in the night. "I'm building something new."

He reached into the darkness, and the bloodvine's tendrils followed, wrapping around his arm like a second skin. Together, they reached into the abyss.

The first creature came quickly—a shade, drawn by the warmth of the farm. The bloodvine's thorns pierced it before it could react, dissolving it into a stream of dark energy that flowed into the plant's stalk. The flower pulsed, the eye widening with pleasure.

Another creature followed. Then another. The bloodvine grew stronger with each feeding, its tendrils extending further into the darkness, its roots spreading beneath the farm's foundations.

Elder Chen was shouting something, but Li Wei couldn't hear him anymore. The voice of the plant filled his mind, drowning out everything else.

*More. More.*

And then the voice changed.

It wasn't just the plant anymore. There was another presence, old and vast, pressing against the edges of his consciousness. A voice that spoke in images rather than words.

*A farm for the abyss. A rancher who understands.*

*Come closer, child of the old blood. Let me show you what your great-grandfather saw.*

The world spun. The nursery dissolved around him, replaced by a vision of a vast landscape—fields of black crops stretching to an endless horizon, skies of purple and gold, creatures of impossible shapes working the soil alongside humans. At the center stood a figure he recognized from the book's illustrations.

His great-grandfather.

The old man turned, and Li Wei saw his face—kind eyes, a weary smile, hands stained dark red from a lifetime of working the abyss soil.

*You found my book,* the vision said. *You planted the seed. You're further along than I ever was.*

*But the harvest is coming, and you're not ready.*

*Not yet.*

The vision shattered, and Li Wei was back in the nursery, gasping for breath

title: Chapter 12
content:
CHAPTER 12: The Roots Beneath

The blood-vine tightened around my wrist.

I stared at the creature before me—if you could call it that. It stood seven feet tall, its body a shifting mass of abyss-touched flesh and what looked like petrified wood. Two hollow pits where eyes should be stared directly at me, and from those pits, pale white roots writhed like worms.

Behind me, Chen Wei had stopped screaming. She was making a sound now like a kettle about to boil.

"Don't run," I whispered. "Whatever happens, don't run."

The creature took a step forward. The ground rippled where its foot landed, and I felt something deep in the soil respond—a vibration that traveled up through my blood-vine and into my bones.

I understood.

This thing wasn't just a monster. It was a gardener. A farmer of the abyss.

The blood-vine pulsed against my skin, and I saw images flash behind my eyes: fields of black grain stretching into infinity, harvested by things that had never known sunlight. Roots that drank not water but suffering. Crops that fed not bodies but the hunger between worlds.

"You feel it," the creature spoke.

Its voice didn't come from its mouth—it didn't have one. The words resonated directly in my skull, like a bell struck inside my brain.

"You carry the seed of the First Gardener."

I looked down at my hands. The blood-vine had crawled up both arms now, forming a lattice of red and green. My great-grandfather's book burned in my pocket, literally—I could feel heat searing through the fabric.

"I don't know what that means," I said.

"Liar."

The creature raised one arm, and the ground exploded between us. Roots—not mine, *theirs*—burst from the soil like spears. They curved in the air, forming a cage around Chen Wei and me.

"Your kind abandoned this place centuries ago. You sealed the breaches and pretended the abyss didn't exist. But we remember. We remember the taste of your fear, the warmth of your blood. It sustained us."

Chen Wei grabbed my shoulder. Her hand was shaking.

"What is it? What does it want?"

"It wants to eat us," I said. "But first, it wants to talk."

The creature tilted its head—a gesture that might have been amusement in some other context. "Clever. The blood remembers its source. You have been touched by the First Gardener's legacy. That means you have a choice."

"Let me guess. Join you or die?"

"Join us or become fertilizer."

The roots around us began to move, slithering closer. I could feel them now, the same way I felt the blood-vine. They were alive, connected to something vast and hungry beneath the village.

I reached into my pocket and pulled out the book. The cover was hot enough to blister my palm, but I didn't let go. Three pages had turned black since I started reading this morning. Three pages, three new things I knew about the abyss.

The creature recoiled.

"Where did you get that?"

"My great-grandfather left it for me. Along with a field and a warning."

I opened the book to the first black page. The words had changed since I last looked—they were shifting, rearranging themselves like living things. But I could still read them.

*To plant in the abyss, one must first become the soil.*

The blood-vine tightened. Pain shot through my arms, up my shoulders, into my chest. I felt something inside me crack open—a door I didn't know existed.

The creature screamed.

Not in pain. In recognition.

"You don't know what you've awakened," it hissed. "That book is not a gift. It's a curse. The First Gardener didn't save this world—he condemned it. He planted the seeds that would grow into the abyss itself."

Chen Wei was crying now. I could hear it in her breath, feel it in the way she pressed against my back.

"I don't care about your history," I said. "I care about the people in this village. I care about the field I'm supposed to farm. And I care about not becoming monster food."

"You already are monster food. You just don't know it yet."

The creature lunged.

I didn't think. I just moved.

The blood-vine responded to my intention, shooting out from my arms like whips. They caught the creature mid-charge, wrapping around its wooden flesh. The impact threw me backward, but I kept my grip on the book.

The creature howled.

"You cannot bind what grows in darkness!"

It ripped free, tearing through the blood-vine like paper. But I saw something I hadn't expected—where the vine had touched, the creature's flesh was smoking. The abyss-touched wood was blackening, cracking, *dying*.

My blood was poison to it.

Maybe that's what the book meant. Maybe becoming the soil meant letting the abyss grow through me, so I could kill it from the inside.

Or maybe I was about to die in a field in the middle of nowhere, next to a woman I barely knew, fighting a monster that had probably forgotten more about farming than I would ever learn.

The creature paused. It looked at the smoking wound on its arm, then back at me.

"The blood of the First Gardener," it whispered. "After all these centuries. You actually carry it."

"I'm starting to think that's a bad thing."

"No." The creature's voice changed. The hunger was gone, replaced by something colder. More calculating. "It means you're the key. The lock. The door."

It turned and walked away.

Just like that.

The roots retreated into the ground. The darkness around us began to thin. Chen Wei collapsed to her knees, gasping for air.

I stayed standing, mostly because I couldn't feel my legs.

"What... what just happened?" she managed.

"I don't know."

"You don't know? That thing almost killed us, and you don't know?"

I looked down at the book in my hands. The heat had faded, but the black pages were still there. Three of them. Three secrets.

"I know I'm supposed to farm here," I said. "I know my great-grandfather left me this book for a reason. And I know that creature is afraid of something."

"It didn't seem afraid."

"It was terrified. That's why it left."

Chen Wei stared at me. Her face was streaked with tears and dirt, but her eyes were sharp. Calculating.

"What are you going to do?"

I looked at the field. The crops were gone, destroyed by the creature's emergence. But the soil was still there. The blood-vine was still there. And somewhere beneath us, the abyss was waiting.

"I'm going to plant something."

"Plant what?"

I opened the book to the second black page. The words had settled now, forming a single sentence:

*The abyss grows where the gardener's blood is spilled.*

I cut my palm on a rock. The blood dripped onto the soil, and the ground began to shake.

"I'm going to plant myself."

The soil split open.

Roots—not the creature's, not the blood-vine's, but something new—burst from the earth and wrapped around my legs. They were warm. Alive. And they were *mine*.

Chen Wei screamed. I didn't.

I could feel it now—the connection I'd been missing. The thing that made my great-grandfather the First Gardener. He hadn't fought the abyss. He'd grown it. Nurtured it. Made it his own.

The roots pulled me into the ground.

I didn't resist.

The last thing I saw before the earth closed over my head was the book, still clutched in my hand, its pages glowing with a light that came from somewhere deeper than fire.

Then everything went dark.

And I began to grow.

title: Chapter 13
content:
CHAPTER 13: The Warden's Price

The scream came from beneath the farm.

Li Wei dropped his hoe and ran. Three weeks of careful expansion, of coaxing strange plants from the gray soil, of learning which abyss creatures could be herded and which would eat him—all of it flashed behind his eyes as he sprinted toward the sound.

The entrance to the lower caverns gaped like a wound in the earth. He'd sealed it with stones after the first week, after exploring far enough to realize the abyss went deeper than his farm could ever reach. But the stones were scattered now, pushed aside by something large.

He grabbed his torch from its bracket and descended.

The tunnel curved down through rock that seemed to breathe. Faint bioluminescence pulsed from veins in the stone, casting shifting shadows that played tricks on his eyes. The scream had come from his newest excavation, where he'd been trying to expand the glowing moss farm.

He found the body first.

One of the small herbivores he'd domesticated—creatures he'd named "moss runners" for their habit of scurrying through his fungal beds—lay torn apart. Not eaten, he realized. Torn. The wounds were precise, almost surgical. Something had wanted to see what was inside.

Then he found the message.

Scratched into the stone wall, still glowing faintly with residual abyss energy: "THE WARDEN WANTS HIS TRIBUTE. THREE DAYS."

Li Wei's blood went cold.

He'd heard the name in fragments. The Abyss Warden. The creatures in the lower tunnels whispered it when they thought he couldn't understand. The glowing mushrooms, when he attuned to them, sometimes showed him images of a figure made of shadow and teeth. He'd assumed it was local mythology, the kind of stories that grew in dark places.

Now someone—or something—had come up from the deep to deliver a message.

"You're supposed to be dead."

The voice came from behind him. Li Wei spun, knife already in his hand.

A woman stood in the tunnel entrance. She was young, maybe mid-twenties, with dirt-caked clothes that had once been fine. Her face was thin, her eyes too bright in the dim light. She carried a makeshift spear made from sharpened bone.

"You're the delivery driver," she said. "The one the surface threw down."

"You know about the surface?" Li Wei kept his knife ready. "You're from up there?"

"I was." She laughed, but there was no humor in it. "Six months ago. The Church of the Light found out I could sense abyss energy. They said I was corrupted. Blessed. Whatever word they use for people they want to disappear."

"The Church threw you down here?"

"Straight into the Warden's territory." She gestured at the message on the wall. "He let me live. Said I could be useful. I've been running messages for him ever since."

Li Wei studied her. She was thin enough that the bones of her wrists stood out like ridges. But there was a hardness in her posture that spoke of survival.

"Why are you telling me this?"

"Because he's going to kill you." She stepped closer. "The Warden doesn't share. This farm you're building, these creatures you're taming—they're in his domain. He's been watching since you arrived. I think he was curious at first. Now he's decided you're a threat."

"Then why warn me?"

She was close enough now that he could see the scars on her face. Old ones, long healed. And newer ones, too fresh to have fully formed.

"Because I've seen what he does to people who refuse him. And I've seen what happens to people who serve him." Her voice dropped. "I'd rather die fighting than live as his puppet."

The tunnel shook.

Dust rained from the ceiling. Deep below them, something rumbled—not an earthquake, but the sound of immense weight shifting. The abyss energy in the walls pulsed brighter, then dimmed.

"He's coming," she said. "He feels you resisting."

"We need to get to the surface."

"There is no surface. Not for us." She grabbed his arm. "But we can run. I know tunnels he doesn't watch. Places even he fears."

Li Wei pulled free. "I'm not leaving my farm."

"Your farm is dead. Everything you've built—he'll tear it apart looking for you."

"Then I'll tear him apart first."

She stared at him like he'd grown a second head. "You don't understand. The Warden isn't like the creatures you've been herding. The abyss made him, and the abyss keeps him alive. You can't kill something that's part of the world itself."

"I'm not trying to kill the world." Li Wei turned and started climbing back toward the surface. "I'm trying to change it."

The farm looked the same when he emerged. The moss glowed softly in its beds. The mushrooms reached toward the cave ceiling. The moss runners were grazing peacefully in their pen, unaware that one of their number had been slaughtered.

But something was wrong.

He felt it before he saw it—a wrongness in the abyss energy that flowed through everything. Like a note played off-key in a song he'd grown to know by heart.

The girl followed him up. She stopped at the entrance and gasped.

"How is this possible?"

"What?"

"The plants. They're... they're accepting the abyss energy. Not just surviving, but thriving." She walked to the nearest mushroom bed and touched one of the glowing caps. "I've never seen anything like this. The Warden's domain is all death and decay. This is..."

"Life," Li Wei finished. "That's what I'm building."

"But the abyss doesn't support life. That's what makes it the abyss."

"The book said otherwise." He pulled out his great-grandfather's journal, the pages worn soft from handling. "It said the abyss was a wound in the world, but wounds can heal. You just need the right medicine."

"That's not what the Church teaches."

"The Church threw me down here. I don't care what they teach."

She was silent for a long moment. Then she said, "There are others."

"Others?"

"People like us. Abyss-touched, thrown down to die. Some survived. Some serve the Warden. Some hide in the deep places, hoping he forgets about them." She met his eyes. "If you can really grow things here, if you can really heal the abyss... they'll want to know."

"How many?"

"Dozens. Maybe hundreds. It's hard to count when everyone's trying not to be eaten."

The ground shook again. Stronger this time.

"He's closer," she said. "We need to decide. Fight or run."

Li Wei looked at his farm. Three weeks of work. Three weeks of coaxing life from death, of learning the strange rules of this underground world. He'd started with nothing but a book and a vine that grew from blood.

Now he had something worth protecting.

"Fight," he said. "But not stupid. You said you know tunnels he fears. Where do they lead?"

"Deep. Deeper than anything you've seen. There are places down there where even the Warden won't go. Places where the abyss is so thick it drives things mad."

"Show me."

She shook her head. "If we go that deep, we might not come back."

"We're not going to stay." Li Wei grabbed a sack and started filling it with supplies. Dried moss, mushroom spores, the seeds he'd been cultivating from his strongest plants. "We're going to find those others you mentioned. Build an army."

"An army of abyss-touched misfits?"

"An army of farmers." He slung the sack over his shoulder. "If I can teach them to grow things, to heal the abyss instead of fighting it... we can starve the Warden out. Cut off his power at the source."

The girl stared at him like he was insane. Maybe he was.

"You don't even know my name," she said.

"Li Wei."

"Kara." She held out her hand. "If we're going to die together, we should at least be formal about it."

He took her hand. Her grip was stronger than he expected.

"We're not dying today," he said. "We're planting seeds."

They moved fast through the tunnels. Kara knew the way—years of running messages for the Warden had taught her every twist and turn. The abyss energy grew thicker as they descended, pressing against Li Wei's skin like a physical weight.

The book in his pocket grew warm.

He pulled it out as they paused to catch their breath. The pages were glowing, faint lines of light tracing patterns he'd never seen before. When he opened it, new words had appeared:

"The Warden is not the enemy. The Warden is a symptom. Cut off the head, and two will grow in its place. Heal the wound, and the head will wither."

"I think I understand," Li Wei said slowly. "The Warden is like a scab. He forms over the wound to protect it, but he also prevents healing. If I try to kill him directly, I'll just make things worse."

"So what do we do?"

"We heal the wound. We make the abyss healthy again. And when there's nothing left for the Warden to feed on..." He snapped his fingers. "He starves."

"That could take years."

"Then we'd better get started."

They heard the Warden before they saw him.

A sound like grinding stone, like teeth scraping together. The tunnel ahead of them went dark—not the absence of light, but the absence of everything. A void that swallowed sight and sound.

"He's found us," Kara whispered.

Li Wei stepped forward, his vine-whip already growing from his palm. The blood-red plant responded to his will, lengthening and thickening as he fed it energy from the book.

"I'm not running anymore," he said. "If he wants my farm, he'll have to take it from me."

The void laughed.

It wasn't a sound that came from a throat. It was the abyss itself laughing, a vibration that shook the ground and made Li Wei's teeth ache.

"Little farmer," the void said. "You think you understand the abyss. You think your book and your plants can change what I am."

"Show yourself."

"No. I am not a thing to be seen. I am the space between things. The darkness that waits at the bottom of every hole. You cannot fight me because I am what you fight against."

Li Wei's vine cracked like a whip, slicing through the void. For a moment, he saw something—a shape made of shadows, with too many limbs and eyes that were never in the same place twice.

Then it was gone.

And the tunnel collapsed.

Stone fell like rain, cutting them off from the path ahead. Li Wei grabbed Kara and pulled her back as the ceiling came down. When the dust settled, the tunnel was sealed.

"Another way," he said. "There has to be another way."

"There is." Kara's voice was shaking. "But we won't like it."

"Tell me anyway."

"The Forsaken Warrens. The deepest part of the abyss, where even the Warden doesn't go. If we go through there, we can come up behind his territory. But the things that live in the Warrens..."

"Things worse than the Warden?"

"Things that made the Warden run away."

Li Wei looked at the sealed tunnel. Then at the book in his hands, glowing with new words he hadn't read yet. Then at the vine growing from his palm, pulsing with life in a place where life shouldn't exist.

"Lead the way," he said.

As they turned toward the deeper

title: Chapter 14
content:
CHAPTER 14: The Roots That Bind

The vine wrapped around my forearm pulsed with a warmth that had nothing to do with body heat. Three days had passed since I'd pulled it from the abyss soil, and it had grown from a single tendril into a coiled bracelet of dark green that never seemed to stop moving. Even now, as I stood at the edge of my tiny farm plot, I could feel it reaching toward something underground.

"You're staring again," Chen Wei said from behind me. The old farmer had appeared at dawn, as he had every morning since the incident with the corrupted grain. "That thing's going to eat you alive if you keep treating it like a pet."

"It's not eating me." I flexed my arm, watching the vine tighten and relax in response. "It's... communicating."

"Communicating." He spat to the side. "It's a weed from the abyss, boy. The only thing it's communicating is how fast it wants to kill you."

I didn't bother arguing. Chen Wei had been farming the surface for forty years, and his worldview was carved from hard soil and harder seasons. He couldn't understand what I'd felt when my blood first touched that black earth—the pulse of something ancient waking up, stretching, *recognizing* me.

The book from my great-grandfather had been useless on the specifics. Most of its pages were still blank, filling in only when I encountered something new. But the entry on abyss flora had appeared the night after I planted the vine, and its words were burned into my memory: *The roots of the abyss remember the blood of the first cultivator. Feed them, and they will feed you. Starve them, and they will take what they need.*

Charming.

"Zhang Wei!" The shout came from the village gate. Captain Liu was jogging toward us, his armor clanking with each step. He looked like he hadn't slept in a week. "You need to come. Now."

"What happened?"

"Another sinkhole. This one's different." He grabbed my arm without waiting for an answer. The vine reacted instantly, coiling tighter, and I felt a spike of something like anger pulse through it. "The ground just opened up last night. Swallowed three houses."

"Anyone inside?"

Liu's face went pale. "They're still there. We can hear them."

The sinkhole was on the eastern edge of the village, where the soil had always been thin over the abyss. But this wasn't just a collapse. As we approached, I saw that the edges of the hole were perfectly circular, as if someone had used a giant cookie cutter on the earth. The houses inside hadn't fallen—they were still standing, perched on a column of dirt that rose from the darkness below.

A crowd had gathered at the rim, throwing ropes and calling down to the trapped families. I pushed through to the edge and looked down.

The abyss wasn't empty anymore.

Roots. Thousands of them, twisting up from the darkness like pale serpents. They wrapped around the column of earth, pulsing with a sickly green light. And at the bottom, where the light faded into absolute black, I could see movement. Something big.

"Your vine," Chen Wei whispered behind me. "It's glowing."

I looked down at my arm. The vine had unwound and was stretching toward the sinkhole, its tip quivering like a dog catching a scent. The green light from the roots below matched its glow perfectly.

"They're the same," I said. "The same species."

"Then burn it," Liu said. "Cut it off and burn it before it spreads."

"No." The word came out before I could think. "It's connected to me. If you burn it, you might kill me too."

That was a lie. I didn't know if it was true. But the vine had saved my life twice already—first from the corrupted grain, then from the abyss creatures that had tried to drag me down during my first night of farming. I wasn't ready to give that up.

"Then what do you suggest?" Liu's hand was on his sword. "We have families trapped down there, and whatever's at the bottom is getting closer to them by the minute."

I looked at the roots again. They were climbing higher, wrapping tighter around the column. The trapped families were screaming now, their voices echoing up from the hole. And at the bottom, the movement was becoming more frantic, more directed.

The vine on my arm pulsed. Once. Twice. Then it started to pull.

Not hard, but insistent. Like a dog on a leash, trying to lead me somewhere. I let it guide my arm, watching as it pointed toward a specific spot on the sinkhole's edge. The earth there was cracked, and through the gap, I could see a single root—thinner than the others, almost dead-looking.

"Give me a knife," I said.

"What?"

"A knife. Now."

Someone handed me a farming sickle. I knelt at the crack and pressed the blade against the thin root. The vine on my arm went rigid, and I felt a clear message: *Wait. Not yet.*

I waited.

The sun climbed higher. The screams from below grew hoarse. Liu was pacing, his men arguing about ropes and harnesses and whether they should just collapse the whole thing and dig everyone out. Chen Wei kept muttering about burning everything and starting over.

And then, at noon, the thin root twitched.

It was subtle—a single pulse of green light that traveled from the tip to the darkness below. But in that moment, every root in the sinkhole stopped moving. The ones climbing the column froze. The ones writhing at the bottom went still. Even the movement in the deep darkness paused.

The vine on my arm relaxed.

I didn't hesitate. I brought the sickle down and severed the thin root in one clean cut.

The effect was immediate. A shriek rose from the abyss—not human, not animal, but something that vibrated in my bones and made my teeth ache. The roots below spasmed, then went limp. The column of earth groaned, and I watched in horror as it began to crumble.

"Pull them up!" Liu shouted. "Now!"

The ropes went taut. The trapped families were hauled up as their platform disintegrated beneath them. I caught a glimpse of a child's face, streaked with tears, before she was yanked over the edge and into her mother's arms.

The column fell. The roots sank into the darkness. And the sinkhole began to fill with something black and viscous that bubbled and steamed in the sunlight.

"Abyss oil," Chen Wei said, his voice hollow. "We need to seal this. Now. Before it reaches the water table."

I looked at my arm. The vine had wrapped itself into a tight coil, and its green light had faded to a dull brown. It felt exhausted. Used up. But it had worked.

For now.

"How did you know?" Liu asked, his voice low. "How did you know cutting that root would stop them?"

I didn't have an answer. Not one that made sense. The vine had told me, but how could I explain that I was taking orders from a plant?

"I didn't," I said. "I guessed."

Liu stared at me for a long moment, then nodded slowly. "We need more farmers. People who can work the abyss soil without dying. People who can control these... things."

"I'm not sure I can control it."

"Better than the alternative." He gestured at the sinkhole, now a pool of bubbling black death. "Because if the abyss keeps spreading, there won't be any surface left to farm."

That night, I sat alone in my hut, staring at the book by candlelight. The vine had recovered some of its color, and it was moving again—restless, hungry. I'd fed it a handful of abyss soil mixed with my blood, and it had grown noticeably thicker in response.

The book had new pages now. Not many, but enough.

*The vine chooses its host based on compatibility. Not all blood will wake the root. Not all wills can command it. You have been chosen. This is not an honor. It is a burden.*

*The root remembers its origin. It will seek to return. You must decide if you will follow it or fight it.*

*There is no third option.*

"What does that mean?" I asked aloud. The vine pulsed, and I felt a whisper of response—not words, but images. A vast darkness. A beating heart made of stone. A web of roots that stretched across an entire world.

Below. Everything was below.

The candle flickered. The shadows in the corners of my hut grew longer. And somewhere in the darkness beneath my floorboards, something scratched at the earth.

I looked down. The vine had extended a single tendril through a crack in the wooden planks, reaching toward the soil below. It was trembling, vibrating with something that felt like anticipation.

Or hunger.

"Not tonight," I said, pulling it back. "We need to be smart about this."

The vine resisted for a moment, then coiled back around my wrist. But I could feel its frustration, its impatience. It had tasted the abyss today, and it wanted more.

I didn't sleep that night. I sat by the window, watching the stars, feeling the vine writhe against my skin. And when dawn finally broke, I saw something that made my blood run cold.

The sinkhole was gone. In its place was a patch of black soil, perfectly circular, from which a single green shoot had emerged. It looked almost exactly like the vine on my arm.

But it was ten feet tall.

And it was growing.

title: Chapter 15
content:
CHAPTER 15: The Harvest That Screamed

The sun hadn't risen yet when the first tremor shook Li Wei's shack.

He jerked awake, hand already reaching for the pitchfork propped against the wall. The crude iron weapon felt flimsy in his calloused grip—a farmer's tool, not a soldier's. But after three weeks in the Abyss, he'd learned that farming tools could kill just as dead as swords.

Another tremor. Stronger this time. Dust trickled from the ceiling.

"Easy, girl." Li Wei pressed his palm flat against the soil where the crimson vine had anchored itself overnight. It had grown again—thicker now, almost as wide as his wrist, its thorny length stretching from the corner of his shack through a hole in the roof toward the surface world above.

The vine pulsed. Warm. Alive.

Li Wei had stopped questioning how a plant could feel like a heartbeat. The Abyss didn't play by surface rules. Three weeks ago, he'd been a delivery driver in Chengdu, dodging traffic and cursing customers who ordered bubble tea at 2 AM. Now he was a farmer in hell, growing crops from soil that drank blood like water.

The third tremor didn't stop.

It built into a low rumble that vibrated through his bones. Li Wei grabbed his coat—a thick leather thing stitched from abyss-beast hide—and shoved open the door.

The sky was wrong.

Above the crater rim that marked the boundary between Abyss and surface, the clouds had gathered into a perfect spiral. Red lightning flickered at its center, illuminating the wasteland in stuttering flashes. Li Wei's crops—rows of Crimson Grain stalks, their heads heavy with blood-red seeds—swayed in a wind that smelled of copper and rot.

"Dammit." He ran.

The field stretched a hundred meters from his shack, carved out of the Abyss's toxic soil through sheer stubbornness. He'd lost fourteen seedlings in the first week alone. Another eight to the burrowing worms that surfaced only at night. But the ones that survived had grown fast. Too fast.

The first stalk he reached was already splitting.

Not from the tremors. From the inside.

Li Wei dropped to his knees, pressing his hands against the fibrous stalk. It was hot. Boiling. Through the green skin, he could see shapes moving—bulbous, pale things that squirmed against the plant's flesh.

"What the hell are you?"

The stalk split open with a wet crack.

What spilled out wasn't seeds.

It was a grub. Fat as his thumb, translucent white, with a single black eye that blinked at the starless Abyss sky. Then another. Then a dozen more. They poured from the ruptured stalk like water from a broken pipe, tumbling onto the black soil and immediately burrowing down.

Li Wei scrambled backward, heart hammering.

The other stalks were splitting too. All of them. The air filled with wet tearing sounds as his entire harvest—three weeks of sweat and blood and desperate hope—exploded into writhing white larvae that vanished into the earth.

"No, no, no—"

Something grabbed his ankle.

He looked down. A grub had surfaced, its mouth—a circular ring of needle-thin teeth—already clamped onto his boot. The leather hissed where its saliva touched. Li Wei stomped, grinding it into the soil, but three more took its place. Then five. Then a wave of them, crawling up his legs, biting through his pants, seeking flesh.

He ran.

The shack's door slammed shut behind him. He bolted it with shaking hands, then pressed his back against the wood, breathing hard. Outside, the hissing of a thousand grubs rose like a tide.

"What did I grow?" His voice cracked.

The crimson vine pulsed against his leg.

He looked down at it—this thing that had sprouted from a single drop of his great-grandfather's blood, this plant that could grow in abyss soil where nothing else survived. He'd trusted it. Nurtured it. Let it anchor itself to his very life force.

And now his fields were crawling with monsters.

The vine pulsed again. Stronger.

And then it moved.

Not with the wind. Not with the tremors. It *coiled*, sliding across the floor like a snake, its tip rising to hover inches from Li Wei's face. A single tendril extended, brushing against his cheek.

Warm. Almost affectionate.

"Don't." Li Wei's hand found the pitchfork. "Don't you dare."

The vine paused. Then, slowly, it turned toward the door. The wood was already splintering under the grubs' assault, their needle-teeth chewing through oak like paper. Li Wei could see them now—a writhing white mass pushing through the gaps, their single black eyes fixed on him.

The vine touched the door.

And the grubs stopped.

Every single one. The hissing died. The chewing ceased. In the sudden silence, Li Wei heard his own heartbeat roaring in his ears.

The vine's tip traced a pattern on the wood. A symbol. One he recognized from his great-grandfather's book—that ancient, water-stained journal that had dragged him into this nightmare in the first place.

The pattern for *harvest.*

"What did you do?" Li Wei whispered.

The grubs began to scream.

Not like insects. Like children. High-pitched, terrified wails that cut through the night like knives. The white mass at his door writhed, twisting in on itself as the grubs began to change. Their translucent skin darkened. Hardened. Their single eyes multiplied into clusters of black orbs that reflected the red lightning above.

They were growing.

Fast.

The first one pushed through the door, and Li Wei saw what it had become. A beetle the size of his fist, its carapace gleaming like polished obsidian, its mandibles dripping with something that smoked when it hit the floor.

Behind it came another. And another.

The vine pulsed against his leg. *Satisfied.*

"You turned my harvest into..." Li Wei couldn't finish the sentence. He watched in horror as the beetles swarmed the field, not attacking each other, not fleeing—just *waiting.* Standing in perfect rows where his grain stalks had been.

Like crops.

The vine tugged at his pant leg, pulling him toward the door. Li Wei resisted, but the grip was iron. It dragged him forward, through the swarm, into the field where the beetles stood motionless under that spiraling sky.

One of them—the largest, its carapace marked with crimson veins—stepped forward. Its mandibles clicked, and Li Wei felt the vibration through the soil. A communication. A question.

*What now?*

The vine wrapped around his wrist, warm and pulsing, and Li Wei understood with sickening clarity.

This wasn't just a plant.

It was a rancher.

And he was its host.

---

The sun still hadn't risen when the hunters came.

Li Wei saw them first as silhouettes on the crater rim—five figures in leather armor, carrying weapons that gleamed with abyss-forged steel. Surface dwellers. The first he'd seen since falling into this hell.

"By the Deep," one of them breathed. "What is that?"

Li Wei looked down at himself. At the beetles that surrounded him in perfect formation. At the vine that had crawled up his arm, its tendrils embedded in his skin, pulsing with his heartbeat.

"I'm fine," he called up, his voice steadier than he felt. "I have control."

The lead hunter—a woman with gray-streaked hair and a scar across her throat—nocked an arrow. "Control? Boy, you're standing in a field of Abyss Born. Those things should have been culled at birth."

"They're mine." Li Wei stepped forward. The beetles parted around him like water around a stone. "I grew them."

"You *grew* them?" The hunter's bow didn't waver. "Nothing grows in the Abyss. Not real crops. Not these monsters. Whatever you've done, you've broken the seal."

"What seal?"

The woman's eyes went wide. "You don't know. You actually don't know." She lowered her bow slowly, her face shifting from hostility to something worse. Pity. "The Abyss isn't just a wasteland, boy. It's a prison. And you've just planted a garden over the lock."

The vine pulsed. The beetles clicked.

And beneath Li Wei's feet, the ground began to crack.

title: Chapter 16
content:
CHAPTER 16: The Market's Dark Harvest

The morning sun painted Chengdu's skyline in shades of amber, but I barely noticed it anymore. The vine had grown again overnight—another meter of crimson-streaked green that now coiled around my apartment's load-bearing pillar like a lover's embrace. It pulsed with that same rhythmic light, synced to my heartbeat.

I didn't sleep anymore. Didn't need to. The abyss had made sure of that.

"You're staring again," Lao Zhang said, his voice crackling through my phone's speaker. "Focus, Chen Wei. The market opens in two hours."

I blinked, realizing I'd been tracing the vine's patterns with my finger. "Sorry. Still getting used to having a plant that eats darkness growing in my living room."

"It doesn't eat darkness. It consumes abyssal energy. There's a difference."

"Is there, though?"

Silence. Then: "Just get to the market early. The merchants are getting nervous. Word spreads fast in Chengdu's underworld."

I grabbed my delivery bag—the same one I'd been carrying when I fell into the abyss three weeks ago. It felt heavier now, weighted with vials of darkrooth extract and bundles of glowmoss I'd harvested from the vine's latest bloom. The abyss had its own economy, and I was learning to work it.

The morning streets were already thick with scooters and electric bikes, delivery drivers weaving through traffic like schools of fish. I used to be one of them. Now I rode an invisible line between two worlds, carrying cargo that would make their hair stand on end.

My phone buzzed. A message from an unknown number: *"The abyss remembers what you took from it."*

I deleted it without responding. The threats had started three days ago, right after I'd sold my first batch of glowmoss to a herbalist in the Old Quarter. Someone knew what I was doing. Someone was watching.

The market was a maze of canvas awnings and steel carts tucked between high-rise apartment blocks. Smells of frying baozi and roasting tea leaves mixed with the metallic tang of abyssal goods. I found my usual spot—a corner stall wedged between a fortune teller and a man selling dried scorpion wine.

"You're late," the fortune teller said without looking up from her cards. "The Red Lotus sent someone yesterday. Asking questions."

My blood went cold. "What kind of questions?"

"The kind that get people killed." She finally met my eyes. Hers were the color of old jade, pupils like pinpricks. "They want to know who's been selling surface-grown abyssal herbs. Their supply chain is collapsing. Prices are dropping. Someone's very unhappy."

I set out my wares: glass vials containing darkrooth extract that shimmered like oil on water, bundles of glowmoss that cast faint blue light on the concrete, and a new product I'd been cultivating—abyssal honey from the vine's nectar, thick and black as tar.

"The honey's new," the fortune teller observed. "What does it do?"

"Heals abyssal burns. One drop on the wound, three seconds to close." I'd tested it on myself. The scar on my arm where a shadow creature had clawed me was already fading.

She nodded slowly. "Interesting. How much?"

"Fifty per vial. Two hundred for bulk."

"A bargain. You know that, right? You could charge ten times that in the right circles."

"I'm not looking for the right circles. I'm looking to survive."

The morning passed in a blur of transactions. Word spread fast—the honey sold out in two hours, followed by the darkrooth extract. By noon, I'd made more money than I'd earned in a month of delivery runs. But the fortune teller's warning gnawed at me.

The Red Lotus. I'd heard the name whispered in the abyss. A syndicate that controlled the flow of abyssal goods across Chengdu's underworld. They operated from shadows, pulling strings that connected government officials, crime bosses, and the strange beings that crawled up from the depths.

And now they had my scent.

My phone buzzed again. Same unknown number: *"Meet us at the Bamboo Pavilion. 2 PM. Come alone."*

I stared at the screen, my thumb hovering over the block button. But I didn't press it. I needed to know who was watching. What they wanted. How much they knew.

"Bad news?" the fortune teller asked.

"Invitation."

"Those are the worst kind."

The Bamboo Pavilion was a teahouse in the wealthy district, all polished wood and paper screens, with a courtyard garden that cost more per square meter than my entire apartment. The hostess recognized me—or rather, the delivery bag I carried—and led me to a private room without a word.

Inside, a woman sat at a low table, pouring tea with practiced grace. She was young, maybe mid-twenties, with sharp features and hair pulled back in a severe bun. Her business suit cost more than my scooter.

"Mr. Chen," she said, gesturing to the seat opposite. "Please. The oolong is exceptional."

I didn't sit. "Who are you?"

"Names are complicated in our line of work." She pushed a cup toward me. "But you can call me Lin. I represent certain... interests."

"The Red Lotus."

Her smile didn't waver. "We prefer to think of ourselves as facilitators. The abyss is chaos. We bring order. We ensure that the flow of goods, information, and power remains stable."

"And I'm disrupting that stability."

"You're flooding the market with surface-grown abyssal herbs. Our suppliers can't compete with your prices. Our buyers are starting to ask questions." She took a sip of tea. "You're a problem, Mr. Chen. But problems can be solutions."

"What do you want?"

"Partnership. We provide protection, resources, distribution. You provide the product. We split the profits sixty-forty."

"Sixty for you?"

"Seventy-thirty. I was testing you." She laughed, a sound like breaking glass. "You're sharper than you look. Thirty percent for you, but we cover all risks. The Surface Authorities, the abyss creatures, the other syndicates—they're our problem."

I thought about the vine in my apartment. The blood I'd fed it. The way it responded to my touch, growing faster when I was angry, producing sweeter nectar when I was afraid. The abyss had marked me in ways I was only beginning to understand.

"And if I refuse?"

Lin's smile vanished. "Then you become a different kind of problem. One with a much simpler solution."

"I need time to think."

"You have until sunset." She stood, smoothing her skirt. "The Bamboo Pavilion has excellent security cameras. We'll be watching."

I left without touching the tea.

The walk back to my apartment was a blur of faces and traffic, my mind racing through options that all ended badly. The Red Lotus had resources I couldn't match. Connections that could make me disappear. But giving them control meant losing the vine. Losing my connection to the abyss.

Losing the only thing that made me feel alive.

My phone buzzed as I reached my building. Another unknown number, but different this time: *"Don't trust the Lotus. They serve the Deep Ones. Meet me at the Night Market. Stall 47. Bring the vine."*

I stopped mid-stride. The vine. How did they know about the vine?

I looked up at my apartment window. The lights were on. I never left the lights on.

My heart hammered as I took the stairs two at a time, delivery bag swinging against my hip. The door was unlocked—I always locked it. The air inside was thick with the smell of ozone and something else. Something that reminded me of the abyss.

The vine was gone.

The pillar where it had coiled was bare, the concrete scarred where roots had dug in. My apartment was ransacked—drawers pulled out, mattress slashed, the potted plant I'd inherited from my grandmother shattered on the floor.

But the vine was gone. Stolen.

I sank to my knees, staring at the empty space where my connection to the abyss had grown. Without the vine, I was just a delivery driver again. A nobody with a scar on his arm and a story no one would believe.

Then I saw it.

A single strand of red-streaked vine, no longer than my finger, still attached to a crack in the wall. It pulsed weakly, the light almost gone. I touched it, and felt a jolt of recognition.

The vine wasn't dead. It was hiding.

I pressed my palm against the wall, feeling for the roots I knew extended deeper than any normal plant's. The building's concrete wasn't a barrier—it was a medium. The vine had grown through the walls, spreading like veins through a body, connecting to every apartment, every floor.

I closed my eyes and willed it to return.

The wall groaned. Cracks spiderwebbed across the paint. And then the vine burst through, thicker than before, covered in thorns I'd never seen. It wrapped around my arm, not constricting but supporting, lifting me to my feet.

"You're still here," I whispered.

A pulse of warmth answered. The vine was alive. And it was angry.

I grabbed my delivery bag and headed for the door. The Night Market. Stall 47. Someone had answers. Someone had taken my vine and thought they could control it.

They didn't know what they'd stolen.

They didn't know what I'd become.

But they were about to find out.

As I stepped into the night, my phone buzzed one last time. The fortune teller's number.

"The Red Lotus just put a bounty on your head," she said, her voice tight. "Fifty thousand for your capture. Double for your corpse."

"Then they should have asked for more."

"What are you going to do?"

I looked down at the vine coiled around my arm, its thorns gleaming in the streetlight. "I'm going to show them what the abyss really grows."

The vine pulsed once, twice, and then began to hum—a low vibration that made the windows rattle and the streetlights flicker. Far beneath the city, something stirred in the darkness.

Something that had been waiting for me to call it home.

title: Chapter 17
content:
CHAPTER 17: The Seed That Shouldn't Exist

The screams from the village carried for miles.

Chen Wei sprinted toward the sound, his bloodvine whip crackling at his side. Behind him, Elder Zhao's ragged breathing grew more labored with each step. The old man had insisted on coming, claiming he knew every inch of the abyss soil. Right now, that knowledge meant nothing against whatever was making those sounds.

They found the first body at the village gate.

The man lay face down, his back split open from shoulder to hip. But there was no blood. The wound was clean, almost surgical, and the flesh around it had turned gray and hard. Like stone.

"Don't touch him," Chen Wei said, grabbing Elder Zhao's arm. "Whatever did this, it's still here."

The village of Stone Hollow sat in a natural crater, protected on three sides by cliff faces. Chen Wei had passed through it a week ago, trading salt for abyss weed seeds. It had been quiet then. Too quiet, he now realized.

Now it was silent.

They moved through the empty streets, past doors hanging open, past abandoned cooking fires still smoldering. The abyss weed fields at the center of the village had been trampled, the precious crops rotting in the mud. But that wasn't what made Chen Wei stop cold.

It was the hole.

In the center of the village square, where a well had once stood, there was now a perfectly circular pit. It descended into absolute darkness, maybe a hundred feet across. The edges were smooth, as if the earth had simply ceased to exist.

"Impossible," Elder Zhao whispered. "The abyss can't breach the surface this far in. The barrier—"

"The barrier's gone." Chen Wei pointed at the sky. The shimmering veil that usually protected surface settlements from abyss miasma had vanished. Above them, the clouds had turned the color of bruises. "When did it fall?"

"Three days ago." The voice came from behind them.

Chen Wei spun, whip crackling. A woman stood in the shadow of a collapsed house, clutching a child to her chest. Her clothes were torn, her face covered in dust and dried blood. But her eyes were sharp, watching them with the focus of someone who had survived by staying alert.

"The barrier fell three days ago," she repeated. "First it went thin, like looking through dirty glass. Then it just... popped. Like a bubble."

"The abyss creatures," Elder Zhao said. "They must have—"

"No creatures." The woman stepped forward, and Chen Wei saw the child in her arms was unconscious, breathing shallowly. "It was people. They came in the night, wearing cloaks made from shadows. They carried something in a box. A box made of bone."

Chen Wei's blood ran cold. "What did they do?"

"They opened it." The woman's voice cracked. "They opened the box, and the ground started eating itself. The well collapsed. Half the village fell into that hole before we could run. And then they planted something—"

"Planted what?"

"A seed. But it wasn't like any seed I've ever seen. It was black. Blacker than the abyss sky. They buried it in the hole, and then they left. And ever since..." She looked at the ground beneath her feet. "The earth has been hungry."

As if responding to her words, the ground shuddered.

Chen Wei grabbed Elder Zhao and pulled him back just as a crack split the earth where they'd been standing. The fissure spread, zigzagging through the village square, and from its depths came a sound like grinding stone.

Something was moving down there.

"What did you plant?" Chen Wei shouted at the woman. "What did they put in the ground?"

"I don't know! But it's been growing. Every night, the hole gets bigger. And the people who fell in... we can still hear them. They're still alive down there, but they're not themselves anymore. They call to us. They want us to join them."

The child in her arms stirred, eyes fluttering open. They were solid black. No white, no iris, just pools of darkness that reflected nothing.

"Momma," the child said, and its voice was wrong. It came from everywhere at once. "They're coming. The roots found them."

"Who?" Chen Wei demanded. "Who's coming?"

"You." The child's head turned, its black eyes fixing on Chen Wei. "The seed knows you. It remembers your blood. It's been waiting for you to come back."

Chen Wei's hand went to his chest, where the wound from the corrupted abyss beast had healed weeks ago. The scar was still there, a pale line of tissue that never quite felt like his own skin.

Elder Zhao grabbed his shoulder. "We need to leave. Now. This whole village is compromised."

"No," Chen Wei said. "If there's a seed growing in that hole, I need to see it."

"Are you insane? Your great-grandfather's book said nothing about seeds that eat the earth!"

"That's exactly why I need to see it." Chen Wei pulled the worn leather book from his pack. "The book is incomplete. The final pages are blank. Everything I've learned has been about cultivating abyss plants, about building roots, about creating bridges between worlds. But nothing in here explains how to fight something like this."

He turned to the woman. "Can you walk?"

She nodded, shifting the child in her arms.

"Then get to the nearest outpost. Tell them what happened here. Tell them the abyss is no longer just rising—it's being planted."

The woman hesitated, then turned and ran. Chen Wei watched her disappear between the houses before facing the hole again.

"You're not going down there," Elder Zhao said.

"I have to."

"Then I'm coming with you."

"No." Chen Wei's voice left no room for argument. "If something happens to me, you're the only one who knows the book. The last entries, the symbols—someone has to carry that knowledge."

"Boy, I'm seventy-three years old. I've lived through abyss storms, creature migrations, and two famines. I'm not about to let a twenty-something delivery driver sacrifice himself while I sit on my hands."

Chen Wei met the old man's eyes. "Then at least stay close. If I don't come back in an hour, seal this village. Burn it if you have to."

He turned before Elder Zhao could argue further and approached the hole.

Up close, it was worse than he'd imagined. The edges weren't just smooth—they were polished, as if the earth had been buffed to a glass-like finish. And from the darkness below came a smell he recognized. It was the same smell that had filled the abyss when his blood had first touched that vine. Rich, organic, alive.

He tied his rope to the nearest intact house beam, tested it twice, then lowered himself over the edge.

The descent took longer than expected. The hole went deeper than any well he'd seen, descending past the water table, past the bedrock, into a darkness so complete it felt solid. His torch flickered, struggling against the oppressive black.

Twenty feet down, he saw the first roots.

They grew from the walls, black as oil, pulsing with a slow rhythm. Like a heartbeat. They twitched as he passed, reaching toward him but stopping short, as if held back by some invisible boundary.

Forty feet down, the walls changed. The dirt and rock gave way to something else. Something that looked like flesh.

He touched it with his free hand. It was warm. And it was breathing.

Sixty feet down, the rope went slack.

Chen Wei looked up. The opening above him was now just a pinprick of gray light. Below him, the roots grew thicker, intertwining into a massive knot that filled the bottom of the hole. And in the center of that knot, pulsing like a heart, was a seed.

But not just any seed.

It was the size of a man's head, smooth and black, with veins of crimson running through it. It pulsed with the same rhythm as the roots, and as Chen Wei watched, it split open.

Not to reveal some creature.

To reveal a symbol.

The same symbol that had been burned into the cover of his great-grandfather's book. The same symbol that appeared in his dreams every night. A spiral, twisting inward, surrounded by three circles.

"You found me." The voice came from everywhere and nowhere. It was old. Ancient. Hungry. "I've been growing for so long, waiting for you to find me."

"Who are you?" Chen Wei's voice echoed in the darkness.

"I am the first seed. The one your great-grandfather planted, then tried to destroy. But you can't kill a seed. You can only bury it. And burying something only makes it grow stronger."

The seed pulsed, and the roots around it began to writhe.

"Your blood brought me here. That day in the abyss, when your life touched mine, I tasted you. I've been following that taste ever since. And now that you're here, I can finally finish what I started."

"What are you going to do?"

The seed split wider, and from within came a darkness darker than the abyss. It reached for Chen Wei, tendrils of pure shadow that moved like smoke, like water, like hunger given form.

"Eat you. And through you, eat everything."

Chen Wei didn't think. He moved.

His whip cracked, the bloodvine lashing out to wrap around a root above him. He pulled, launching himself upward just as the shadows closed where he'd been hanging. The root he'd grabbed pulsed, and he felt it try to absorb him, to pull him into its mass.

But his blood was already part of it.

And part of him remembered what it meant to grow.

"Elder Zhao!" he shouted, his voice carrying up the shaft. "Burn it! Burn everything!"

"What about you?!"

"I'll find my own way out!"

The old man's voice came back, faint but determined. "You better!"

The first flames appeared at the top of the hole, orange and hungry, devouring the roots that lined the walls. The seed screamed, a sound that wasn't sound, a vibration that shook Chen Wei's bones.

"You think fire can stop me?" The voice was angry now. "I've been burning for centuries. I've been buried for millennia. Fire is just another form of hunger, and hunger is what I am."

The roots began to move faster, reaching for Chen Wei, trying to drag him down. He slashed at them with his whip, the bloodvine cutting through the black tendrils like a scythe through wheat. But for every one he cut, three more grew.

He climbed, his muscles screaming, his lungs burning from the smoke and miasma. The fire was spreading, chasing him up the shaft, and behind it came the seed's laughter.

"Run, little farmer. Run back to your surface, back to your crops and your walls. But I'll be waiting. I'll always be waiting. Because you can't kill what was never alive. And you can't bury what's already dead."

Chen Wei burst from the hole just as the entire village square collapsed.

He rolled across the ground, coughing, covered in soot and abyss filth. Elder Zhao was there, dragging him away from the growing inferno. Behind them, Stone Hollow burned, the flames reaching toward the bruised sky.

And in the center of the fire, the hole continued to grow.

Chen Wei watched as the earth ate itself, as the flames fell into the darkness, as the seed's laughter faded into a whisper that he could still feel vibrating in his chest.

He looked at his hands. They were shaking.

title: The Bloodvine's New Hunger
content:
The iron taste of blood filled Chen Wei's mouth as he bit down hard on his tongue. The pain kept him focused on the writhing mass of scarlet vines that had breached the farm's outer wall.

"The barrier fell," he whispered, watching red tendrils snake through the broken stones. "How?"

Old Wang's hands trembled as he loaded his crossbow. "That's not normal abyss vine. Look at the color—it's feeding on something alive."

Zhao Ming pressed her back against the grain silo, her harvester's blade dripping with black ichor from earlier skirmishes. "The last incursion left corpses in the ravine. I didn't think they'd decompose this fast."

"They didn't." Chen Wei's voice turned cold. "The vine is draining them directly."

Midsummer's pale light caught the surface of the vines, revealing patterns that made Chen Wei's stomach drop. The same spiral formations from his great-grandfather's book. The same symbols that marked the boundary between abyss and surface.

"Squad leader, we need to burn it," Zhao Ming said. "Before it reaches the crops."

"Burning won't stop this." Chen Wei pulled the leather-bound book from his jacket, flipping to a page he'd memorized but never understood. "There's a passage about 'blood that remembers purpose.' I think this is what it means."

The vine stopped moving.

All three of them froze. The creature—and Chen Wei was certain now it was a creature, not just a plant—had turned its attention toward them. Hundreds of tiny tendrils lifted like snakes tasting air.

"It's listening," Old Wang whispered.

"It's learning," Chen Wei corrected.

The main vine coiled back, then struck forward with terrifying speed. Chen Wei dove left, rolling through mud as the tendril punched through the grain silo like paper. Grain exploded outward in a golden cloud.

"Zhao! Flank it!"

She was already moving, her blade carving an arc through the air. The vine twisted impossibly, avoiding the strike entirely. But the movement exposed its base—a pulsing knot of crimson fibers rooted deep in the earth.

"There." Chen Wei pointed. "That's the heart."

The book's pages rustled in his hands, and he caught a glimpse of his great-grandfather's handwriting: *"To kill the vine, feed it what it cannot consume."*

"What does that mean?" Zhao Ming shouted, dodging another strike.

Chen Wei's mind raced. The vine fed on blood, on the dead. It consumed life force and grew stronger. But what couldn't it consume?

He looked at the crops. At the wheat that had grown from seeds he'd bought with his meager savings. At the vegetables that had sprouted in soil he'd carried from the surface world.

"Salt," he said.

"What?"

"The ocean. The book mentions the great salt waters that separate worlds. The vine comes from the abyss—it's never known salt."

Old Wang's eyes widened. "The supply shed. We've got three barrels of rock salt for preserving meat."

"Buy me time."

Chen Wei sprinted toward the shed, his boots squelching in mud made wet by blood and morning dew. Behind him, he heard Zhao Ming's battle cry and the wet sound of blade meeting vine.

The shed's door had been torn off its hinges. Inside, the salt barrels sat untouched, their wooden lids sealed with wax. Chen Wei grabbed the nearest one and heaved. It weighed at least fifty kilograms, but adrenaline turned his muscles to steel.

He dragged the barrel through the mud, cursing with every step. The vine had circled around, cutting off his direct path to Zhao Ming and Old Wang. They were pinned against the silo, fighting off a dozen smaller tendrils.

"Chen Wei, any time now!" Zhao Ming's voice cracked.

He jammed his knife under the barrel's lid and pried it open. White crystals spilled across the ground. Chen Wei scooped up handfuls and threw them at the nearest vine.

The reaction was immediate.

Where salt touched the red fibers, they sizzled and blackened. The vine shrieked—a sound like metal scraping bone—and withdrew its tendrils.

"It worked!" Old Wang shouted.

But Chen Wei wasn't celebrating. The main vine had stopped its assault, but it was changing. The crimson color deepened to purple, then black. New growths sprouted along its length—thorns that dripped with what looked like venom.

"Great," Zhao Ming muttered. "You made it angry."

"No." Chen Wei stared at the book in his hands. "I made it evolve."

The handwriting continued: *"The bloodvine adapts to every weapon used against it. Fire breeds immunity. Cold breeds resistance. Only the element it cannot comprehend will destroy it utterly."*

"What element?" Zhao Ming demanded.

Chen Wei thought of his great-grandfather's other notes. The ramblings about light that came from within, about seeds that grew in the darkness between worlds, about—

"The abyss itself."

"Are you insane?"

"Maybe." Chen Wei turned to face the creature. "But the book says the vine is a bridge. A connection between two worlds. You can't destroy a bridge by attacking its middle—you have to break one of the anchor points."

Old Wang understood first. "You want to send it back."

"Or pull the surface through to it. Either way, the connection breaks."

Zhao Ming grabbed his arm. "That's suicide. If you open a direct path to the abyss—"

"Then we get the whole abyss instead of just a piece of it." Chen Wei pulled free. "But look at what it's doing. It's growing faster than we can kill it. In an hour, it'll reach the inner farm. In a day, it'll spread to the nearby villages. In a week—"

"Fine." Zhao Ming's jaw tightened. "What do you need?"

Chen Wei opened the book to the final page. There, drawn in ink that seemed to shift and writhe, was a circle divided into three parts. At the center, a single word: *"Harvest."*

"I need the vine's blood. Fresh. And I need you to keep it distracted while I draw the circle."

Old Wang stepped forward. "I can do the drawing. You have better instincts for fighting."

"No." Chen Wei's voice was firm. "The book chose me. It only responds to my blood."

He didn't mention that the previous owner had died drawing this exact circle. That his great-grandfather's final journal entry had been cut off mid-sentence. That the ink on the last page was still wet, as if written moments before Chen Wei had arrived in this world.

Some things were better left unsaid.

The vine lashed out, and Chen Wei dove behind the barrel. Salt crystals scattered through the air, buying him precious seconds. He drew a line in the mud with his finger, then another, forming the first curve of the circle.

"Keep it busy!"

Zhao Ming charged forward, her blade singing through the air. She moved like water, flowing around strikes and countering with precise jabs. Old Wang covered her with crossbow bolts, each shot aimed at the vine's growing number of eyes.

Chen Wei's hands moved faster. The circle took shape, its symbols glowing faintly as he traced them. But the book required more than just the drawing—it needed intent. Focus. A clear image of what he wanted to harvest.

He thought of the abyss. Of the endless darkness that had swallowed so many. Of the creatures that lurked there, waiting for a chance to cross into the surface world.

And he thought of the vine. Not as an enemy, but as a tool. A connection that could be reversed.

"The harvest is the bridge itself," he whispered. "I'm not sending it back. I'm bringing the abyss through."

The circle completed.

The vine screamed.

But it wasn't a scream of pain. It was a scream of recognition. The creature had felt what Chen Wei had done, and it was calling to something deeper, something older.

The ground trembled.

"What did you do?" Zhao Ming shouted, backing away from the circle as light began to leak from its edges.

Chen Wei couldn't answer. His hand was locked to the page, his blood flowing into the book. The words were rewriting themselves, forming sentences he'd never seen before:

*"The rancher who harvests the abyss becomes the abyss. The bridge that carries one world carries both. There is no return from the harvest."*

"I think," Chen Wei said, his voice distant, "I just made a mistake."

The ground split open.

From the crack emerged not vines, but roots. Roots made of pure darkness, drinking the light from the air. They reached for Chen Wei, for the book, for the circle he'd drawn.

And somewhere, deep beneath the farm, something ancient began to stir.

Old Wang grabbed Chen Wei's shoulder and pulled him back just as the roots struck. The book slipped from Chen Wei's grasp, falling into the crack. It disappeared into the darkness.

"Your book!" Zhao Ming cried.

Chen Wei stared at the hole in the ground. The roots were retreating, but the crack remained. A vertical wound in the earth, pulsing with black light.

"It's fine," he said. "I already read it."
